title: Course Substitutions and General Academic Petitions
content:
## About

If you would like to submit a petition to substitute a course for a major or general education requirement, please schedule a meeting with your advisor to complete the **Course Substitution.**

**General Academic Petitions** should only be used for non-course substitution reasons, with a few exceptions. If you need to submit a General Academic Petition for any of the following reasons, please contact a BAC advisor at bac@cpp.edu for further information and guidance:

* Elective/Emphasis/Concentration Declarations if deviating from catalog or curriculum sheet
* Study Abroad (IPC) course substitutions
* Credit from non-regionally accredited institutions (after completion of 20 units at CPP)
* Rescinding a previously submitted petition
* Discontinued student petitions (graduate out of residency)
* Any requests for catalog or specific policy​ exceptions

[### Advice for Students (PDF)](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/documents/advice-for-students-about-grade-appeals.pdf "opens a new window")

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Upcoming Grade Appeal Deadlines

**For Grades Received in Fall 2024/Winter 2025:** March 7, 2025

**For Grades Received in Spring/Summer 2025:** October 10, 2025

**For Grades Received in Fall 2025/Winter 2026:** March 6, 2026

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Grade Appeals Policy and Procedure

Under the provisions of [Executive Order 1037](https://calstate.policystat.com/policy/7949098/latest/), “Grading Symbols, Minimum Standards Governing the Assignment of Grades, Policies on the Repetition of Courses, Polices on Academic Renewal, and Grade Appeals”, students may appeal grades that they consider to be unfair.

The Executive Order governs the assignment of grades by faculty and requires an appeal procedure to ensure that the rights and responsibilities of faculty and students are properly recognized and protected. Occasionally, a circumstance will prevent the assignment of an earned grade or will cause an assigned grade to be questioned by a student. The following policy has been adopted by Cal Poly Pomona to provide the mechanism to deal with such unusual occurrences:

* Course grades assigned by instructors are presumed to be correct. It is the responsibility of the student who appeals an assigned grade to demonstrate:
  1. Clerical error;
  2. Capriciousness in the assignment of the grade;
  3. That a reasonable accommodation for a documented disability was requested and not appropriately provided; or
  4. That the grade was assigned in violation of the [Systemwide Policy Prohibiting Discrimination, Harassment & Retaliation, Sexual Misconduct, Dating & Domestic Violence, & Stalking Against Students](https://calstate.policystat.com/policy/8453516/latest/).

Otherwise, the judgment of the instructor is final.

* A student who believes that a course grade has been assigned inappropriately must take action during the semester following assignment of the grade, unless they allege that the grade was assigned in violation of the Systemwide Policy Prohibiting Discrimination, Harassment & Retaliation, Sexual Misconduct, Dating & Domestic Violence, & Stalking Against Students, in which case action should be taken after resolution of the systemwide procedure for handling such complaints.
* Students must follow the proper steps in the appeal process, observing the time limits for completion of various steps in the process as follows.
* **Please note that per policy, once your degree is awarded, your student record is permanent and no further changes will be made**. Please review the Declaration of Degree Requirements section under Academic Policies in the [University Catalog](https://catalog.cpp.edu/) for more information.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Step 1

The student should communicate with the instructor in writing during the first four full weeks of the semester following the assignment of the grade.

* If the instructor is not a faculty at the time of the appeal, the University shall attempt to contact the instructor on behalf of the student.
* If a grade has been assigned in error, the instructor can quickly correct the error by submitting a change of grade request to the Registrar’s Office Grade Change Request via the online grading system.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Step 2

If the grade dispute is not resolved with the instructor, and the student still intends to appeal the grade, the student must appeal to the next levels as soon as possible, but no later than the fifth full week of the same semester.

In most cases, the student should appeal to:

1. The [chair of the academic department](https://www.cpp.edu/faculty-affairs/about-us/dept-chairs-deans-dir.shtml) that offered the class.
2. The [dean of the college](https://www.cpp.edu/faculty-affairs/about-us/dept-chairs-deans-dir.shtml) that offered the class.
   * If the instructor is a department chair, the student should appeal to the dean of the college that offered the class.
   * If the instructor is a dean, the student should appeal to the Provost.

The person to whom the student appealed will discuss the issue with the instructor and respond to the student, usually within two weeks, but no later than the end of the 6th full week of the semester following the assignment of the grade.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Step 3

If the student is still unsatisfied after receiving the response from the following level of appeal, they may submit a formal grade appeal no later than the end of the 7th full week of the following semester to the University Course Grade Appeal Committee through the Office of Student Success. The University Course Grade Appeal Committee is constituted annually and is comprised of faculty appointed by the Academic Senate, a Student Success representative, a Student Affairs representative, and a student appointed by ASI. This committee will handle grade appeals in a manner compliant with FERPA and HIPAA regulations. Additional information on preparing a written grade appeal is available in the [Advice for Students document (PDF)](../../documents/advice-for-students-about-grade-appeals.pdf).

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Step 4

Shortly after the petition deadline, the Chair of the University Course Grade Appeal Committee will forward the student's statement to the instructor. The instructor will be asked to respond in writing by a specified date (normally within two weeks). Afterwards, the student's statement and the instructor's response will then be reviewed by the entire Grade Appeal committee in a meeting, normally scheduled within two weeks of receipt of the instructor's response.

The Committee will take one of the following actions:

1. Request additional information from the student and/or the instructor.
2. If the University Course Grade Appeal Committee finds that the student has grounds for complaint based on clerical error, caprice, not receiving reasonable accommodation for a documented disability, or a violation of the Systemwide Policy Prohibiting Discrimination, Harassment & Retaliation, Sexual Misconduct, Dating & Domestic Violence, & Stalking Against Students, then the instructor of record will be asked to reevaluate the grade.
   * If the instructor refuses to reevaluate the grade or the instructor's reevaluation does not meet the expectations of the committee, then the chair of the academic department that offered the class shall be asked to find a qualified faculty member with academic training comparable to the instructor of record to evaluate the student's work and assign a grade.
   * If the instructor is a department chair, the dean of the college that offered the class shall be asked to find a qualified faculty member to complete this task; and if the instructor is a dean, the Provost will be asked to find a qualified faculty member to complete this task.
3. Recommend to the instructor that the grade be maintained as given.
4. Call for a formal hearing.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Step 5

When the Committee has made its recommendation, the student will be notified of it in writing, and be given a copy of the instructor’s written response to the student’s statement. This grade appeal procedure may take six to eight weeks to complete, and therefore should finish by the end of the 15th week. The outcome of the formal grade appeal procedure is final; there is no higher level of appeal. However, if there is an allegation of improper procedure, this may be reported to the Provost or their designee.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
## Additional Information

For additional information on preparing a written grade appeal, refer to the [Advice for Students document (PDF)](../../documents/advice-for-students-about-grade-appeals.pdf), or contact the Office of Student Success at osspetitions@cpp.edu.

title: Grade Appeals
content:
written grade appeal, refer to the [Advice for Students document (PDF)](../../documents/advice-for-students-about-grade-appeals.pdf), or contact the Office of Student Success at osspetitions@cpp.edu.

title: Policy on Recording Faculty Lectures
content: # Policy on Recording Faculty Lectures

title: Policy on Recording Faculty Lectures
content:
## California State Polytechnic University, Pomona Policy No: 1416: Taping of Faculty Lectures

Disabled students may tape faculty lectures of any classroom presentation. If students identify themselves as disabled and ask permission to tape lectures or classroom presentations, the instructor may ask them to sign a “limited release statement” that the tapes of lectures would be used only by the student and only for class purposes.

For all other students, instructors may, but are not required to, permit taping, either audio or video, of their lectures or any classroom presentation. In the case of visiting or guest lecturers, the discretion rests with the person actually making the presentation.

If instructors grant permission they should do so in writing, specifying the limitations they may wish to place upon the use and further distribution of the taped material.
section: California State Polytechnic University, Pomona Policy No: 1416: Taping of Faculty Lectures

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
## About Retroactive Withdrawal

Retroactive Withdrawal is a Cal Poly Pomona process that allows students to petition to withdraw from all or some classes in one or more terms after those terms have ended. Students must apply for retroactive withdrawal within one calendar year from the last day of the term they unofficially withdrew from their classes and provide a clear explanation of why they did not withdraw from the university by filing appropriate forms with the Registrar's Office during the term(s) in question.

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content: ## FAQs about Retroactive Withdrawal

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### What is a Retroactive Withdrawal?

The Retroactive Withdrawal process allows students to petition to withdraw from all or some classes in one or more terms after those terms have ended. If permission is granted, the result is that all failing grades (F, WU or IC) for those terms are changed to WX grades. Please see the chart below for a description of the WX grade.

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### Who can apply for a Retroactive Withdrawal?

Students who unofficially withdraw from one or more terms, receive WU grades in ALL coursework taken those terms, and have serious and compelling reasons for the unofficial withdrawal. See the chart below for a description of the WU grade.
section: Who can apply for a Retroactive Withdrawal?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### When can I apply for a Retroactive Withdrawal?

A student has up to one (1) calendar year from the last day of the term in question to apply for a Retroactive Withdrawal. A student does not need to be enrolled at the University at the time that the petition for Retroactive Withdrawal is submitted. The petition form and documentation should be submitted to the Office of Student Success. Deadlines can be found on the Student Success Central Academic Calendar.
section: When can I apply for a Retroactive Withdrawal?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### Once I submit the petition, what happens?

All efforts are made to schedule the Retroactive Withdrawal Committee within a few weeks following the deadline. However, faculty availability determines the time that a meeting can be scheduled so that committee members can consider all Retroactive Withdrawal petitions submitted for that term. In addition, those petitions submitted during the summer term may not be reviewed until the fall term. Once decisions have been rendered, each student is notified in writing approximately one (1) week after the Committee’s decision.
section: Once I submit the petition, what happens?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### How does the review committee decide?

The Cal Poly Pomona Retroactive Withdrawal Committee will give consideration to the following factors:

* The student has provided a clear explanation of why the student did not withdraw from the university by filing the appropriate forms with the Registrar’s Office in a timely manner during the term(s) in question.
* The student has serious and compelling reasons for the student’s unofficial withdrawal, e.g., serious illness or injury to student or family member, death in the family, etc.
* The student has provided documentation of the serious and compelling reasons.
* The student’s overall academic history.
* If the student earned A-F grades during the term(s) in question, a clear explanation of why those grades were not WU grades.
* In the rare case that exceptional circumstances prevent the student from filing the petition within one calendar year of the last day of the term(s) for which the student is requesting retroactive withdrawal, a clear explanation, supported by documentation, of these circumstances.
* Supporting documentation from the student’s major department advisor or from the student’s advisor(s) in support programs such as the Maximizing Engineering Potential Program, the Educational Opportunity Program, and Disability Resource Center.
section: How does the review committee decide?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### What are the steps for submitting a petition for retroactive withdrawal?

* Access the Request for Retroactive Withdrawal e-form [through PolyDocs](https://na3.docusign.net/Member/PowerFormSigning.aspx?PowerFormId=8f6dbaec-6bdd-4cde-95aa-48d490364ef6&env=na3&v=2). If you are unable to access it, contact the Office of Student Success at osspetitions@cpp.edu.
* Review the guidelines on the form carefully. If you have questions about the process, contact the Office of Student Success at osspetitions@cpp.edu.
* Complete, sign and date the form.
* Provide a statement [following the suggestions](../../documents/retroactive-withdrawal-suggestions-for-preparing-your-request_07.08.19.pdf). Use business letter format and address your statement to the Retroactive Withdrawal Committee.
* Provide documentation that supports your statement.
* Before submitting your petition packet, review your packet with your college advisor.
* Submit your completed petition packet by the deadlines posted on the Office of Student Success website.
section: What are the steps for submitting a petition for retroactive withdrawal?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### What happens if my petition is denied for altered or falsified documentation?

The University is committed to maintaining integrity in and outside of the classroom.

If you receive communication that your petition has been denied for insufficient and/or possibly altered documentation, you should resubmit within the timeframe communicated, and ensure the information submitted is accurate, honest, and from the original source. Please review our documentation guidelines for more information.

You may receive additional communication from the Care Center to offer additional support.
section: What happens if my petition is denied for altered or falsified documentation?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### What happens if my petition is denied for insufficient documentation?

If you receive communication that your petition has been denied for insufficient documentation, you should resubmit within the timeframe communicated, and ensure the information submitted is accurate, honest, and from the original source. Please review our documentation guidelines for more information.

You may receive additional communication from the Care Center or the Bronco Advising Center to offer additional support.
section: What happens if my petition is denied for insufficient documentation?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### Can a grade be changed to support a retroactive withdrawal?

No. Grade changes cannot be used as a substitute for the retroactive withdrawal process. A grade change must accurately reflect your demonstrated performance in the course.
section: Can a grade be changed to support a retroactive withdrawal?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
### Where can I get more information?

The Request for Retroactive Withdrawal petition e-form, as well as general information and guidelines, is available on the e-form.
section: Where can I get more information?

title: Retroactive Withdrawal
content:
## Ws, WXs and WUs

Table Explaining Ws, WXs, and WUs

| **Grade** | **What does this grade mean?** | **How do I receive this grade?** | **Notes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| W | This grade means that you withdrew from the class after the Add/Drop Period. | Students can withdraw between the 11th and 20th day of class via BroncoDirect and receive a W. Students with serious and compelling reasons to withdraw after the 20th day through the 12th week of instruction must complete the Withdrawal for Serious and Compelling Reasons form to earn a W.   * Examples of why students may apply: * Work hours changed that conflict with class * Illness (student or close family) * Other circumstances that hinder (but do not prevent) attendance  Not acceptable reasons to apply:   * Failing a class * Don’t need the class to graduate * Never attended class | Students are limited to 18 units of recorded course withdrawals (where the student received a “W”) |
| WX | This grade means that you withdrew from a class after the Add/Drop Period, and the circumstances were serious and compelling, beyond your control. | Students with serious and compelling reasons to withdraw after the 10th day through finals must complete the Withdrawal for Serious and Compelling Reasons form to earn a WX.   The Chair, Dean, and Associate Vice President for the Office of Student Success will evaluate the petition and documentation to determine if a WX is appropriate.   Examples of why students may apply:   * Work conditions change preventing attendance * Serious illness (student or close family) preventing attendance  Students also receive this grade if they have an approved Retroactive Withdrawal. Retroactive Withdrawals are submitted after the term is over and withdraw the student for all classes for a term. | Students are limited to 18 units of recorded course withdrawals (where the student received a “W”)  If a student earns a WX for a course, those units will not count to their 18-unit limit. |
| WU | This grade means that you did not withdraw from the course and failed to complete the course requirements. | Students who do not withdraw from their course and do not meet the course requirements will earn a WU. | WUs are equivalent to Fs for the purposes of grade point averages. |

title: Withdrawal Information
content: ## Frequently Asked Questions

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### Can I drop a course or all of my courses?

* If you drop a course or all of your courses during general registration or Add/Drop Period, the dropped courses will not appear on your transcript. You can find general registration and Add/Drop Period dates on the [Student Success Central Academic Calendar](http://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/academic-calendar/index.shtml).
* If you want to drop a course or all of your courses during the withdrawal period, Ws will appear for those courses on your transcript. You are limited to 18 units of Ws on your transcript. The grade of W does not affect your grade point average (GPA). You can find withdrawal period dates on the [Student Success Central Academic Calendar](http://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/academic-calendar/index.shtml).
* You have the right to petition to receive a WX when withdrawing from a course. A WX is granted when you have demonstrated that your withdrawal is due to serious and compelling reasons beyond your control. A WX does not count toward your 18 units of Ws.
section: Can I drop a course or all of my courses?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### What can I do after the withdrawal period ends?

* After the withdrawal period ends, withdrawals will only be permitted for serious and compelling reasons, such as accident or serious illness, where the cause of withdrawal is due to circumstances clearly beyond your control and the assignment of an Incomplete is not practicable.
* You will need to fill out the Petition to Withdraw for Serious and Compelling Reasons if you wish to drop a course or all of your courses.
section: What can I do after the withdrawal period ends?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### Do I need to submit documentation?

* **All students will be required to submit documentation when submitting a Petition to Withdraw for Serious and Compelling Reasons, regardless of whether they are requesting a W or a WX.**
  + The documentation must be related to the reason why you are petitioning to withdraw.
  + If you are providing medical documentation, it must be on official letterhead and include your name and doctor's contact information. The documentation does not need to disclose a diagnosis.
  + If you are providing photos as documentation, do not submit photos of deceased people or body parts.
section: Do I need to submit documentation?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### What are the steps for submitting a Request for Class Withdrawal for Serious and Compelling Reasons?

* The petition is available above or on the [Registrar’s Office website](https://www.cpp.edu/registrar/forms/forms.shtml). It must be signed electronically by the Department Chair, College Dean, and AVP for Student Success.
* If your request is granted, you will receive Ws or WXs depending on your request and the decisions of the appropriate reviewers.
section: What are the steps for submitting a Request for Class Withdrawal for Serious and Compelling Reasons?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### What happens if I don’t fill out the request but stop attending class(es)?

* If you stop attending class(es) (unofficially withdrawal) and do not follow this procedure, you may receive a grade of F or WU (Withdrawal Unauthorized). F and WU grades are equivalent for the purposes of GPA and grade points.
* The symbol WU indicates that you did not withdraw from the course and also failed to complete course requirements. It is used when, in the opinion of the instructor, you completed assignments or course activities or both were insufficient to make normal evaluation of academic performance possible.
* WU is also assigned if you do not drop a course properly, such as when a student withdraws from a course without authorization (e.g. no approved Request for Class Withdrawal for Serious and Compelling Reasons Form is on file).
* If the appropriate withdrawal form is on file, this WU will be replaced by a W in the Registrar's Office and a W will appear on the final grade sheet returned to the instructor and on your grade report.
section: What happens if I don’t fill out the request but stop attending class(es)?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### What happens if my petition is denied for altered or falsified documentation?

The University is committed to maintaining integrity in and outside of the classroom.

If you receive communication that your petition has been denied for insufficient and/or possibly altered documentation, you should resubmit within the timeframe communicated, and ensure the information submitted is accurate, honest, and from the original source. Please review our documentation guidelines for more information.

You may receive additional communication from the Care Center to offer additional support.
section: What happens if my petition is denied for altered or falsified documentation?

title: Withdrawal Information
content:
### What happens if my petition is denied for insufficient documentation?

If you receive communication that your petition has been denied for insufficient documentation, you should resubmit within the timeframe communicated, and ensure the information submitted is accurate, honest, and from the original source. Please review our documentation guidelines for more information.

You may receive additional communication from the Care Center or the Bronco Advising Center to offer additional support.
section: What happens if my petition is denied for insufficient documentation?

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
## Mission and Vision

Our mission is to cultivate an inclusive academic environment that promotes student success and graduation by providing mentoring, advising, tutoring, and community-building programs and fostering transitional pathways, academic achievement, and holistic development. We strive to empower students to thrive academically and achieve their academic goals by promoting holistic well-being and a sense of belonging. We aim to be the leading force in supporting academic excellence and graduation by creating an inclusive environment where every student's academic potential is realized.

![Students cram for finals during a study session in the University Library ](img/index-aes.png)

content: ## CPP is a Hispanic-Serving Institution (HSI)

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
### ![Map of HSIs across California](../img/access-and-transitions/hsi-map.png)

Read the CSU's [HSI fact sheet (PDF)](../documents/hsi-fact-sheet-accessible.pdf) for more information about HSIs in the CSU system.

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
### What is an HSI?

Definitions of a Hispanic-Serving Institution (HSI) can vary depending on the organization, and include technical and philosophical characteristics.

* **A Hispanic-Serving Institution (HSI) is more than a Hispanic-enrolling one.** While there are many ways a campus can be Hispanic-serving, an HSI is one that creates equity for, and advances the success of, *all*students, and is intentional about its Hispanic/Latino students who represent a notable proportion of the student body.
* For membership purposes, the Hispanic Association of Colleges and Universities ([HACU](https://www.hacu.net/hacu/default.asp "HACU")) defines HSIs as "colleges, universities, or systems/districts where total Hispanic enrollment constitutes a minimum of 25% of the total headcount enrollment."
* Under federal law (Higher Education Opportunity Act, Title V, 2008), an HSI is defined as accredited, degree-granting, public or private nonprofit institutions of higher education with 25%, or more, total undergraduate Hispanic full-time equivalent (FTE) student enrollment.
* Additional criteria exist for federal Title III and Title V HSI grant programs.

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
### HSIs by the Numbers

According to [Excelencia in Education,](https://www.edexcelencia.org/) a not-for-profit organization in Washington, DC, and trusted information source on Latino educational achievement, in 2021-22, there were 571 institutions in 25 U.S. states and Puerto Rico that met the enrollment definition of HSI.

* 571 HSIs enrolled 62% of all Latino undergraduates and represented 19% of all institutions of higher education.

* One-third of HSIs are California campuses and include 21 California State Universities (CSUs), including Cal Poly Pomona. Learn more on the the [CSU website](https://www.calstate.edu/impact-of-the-csu/community/Pages/latino-communities.aspx).

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
## CPP is an Asian American and Native American Pacific Islander-Serving Institution (AANAPISI)

![Map of AANAPISI CSUs in California](../img/aanapisi-map.png)

Read the CSU's [AANAPISI fact sheet (PDF)](../documents/aanapisi-map.pdf) for more information about AANAPISIs in the CSU system.
section: CPP is an Asian American and Native American Pacific Islander-Serving Institution (AANAPISI)

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
### What is an AANAPISI?

Asian American and Native American Pacific Islander-Serving Institutions (AANAPISI) are defined under the Higher Education Act (HEA) as colleges or universities with an undergraduate enrollment that is at least 10 percent Asian American and Native American Pacific Islander. Additionally, at least half of the institution’s degree-seeking students must be low-income.

title: Academic Engagement and Success
content:
### AANAPISIs by the Numbers

According to [Asian Pasific Americans in Higher Education](https://apahenational.org/resources/aanapisi/), funded AANAPISIs represent 32 public four-year, 19 public two-year, and 4 private four-year institutions across the United States.

* AANAPISIs enroll over 40% of all AA&NHPI undergraduates in the nation, playing a critical role in their access to and success in higher education. Funded AANAPISIs enroll over 980,000 total undergraduates in 2023, with nearly 1 in 5 being an AA&NHPI undergraduate.
* 13 California State Universities (CSUs), including Cal Poly Pomona, have the AANAPISI designation. Learn more on the the [CSU website](https://www.calstate.edu/impact-of-the-csu/diversity/csu-asap).

title: Common Read 2020-21
content: # Common Read 2020-21

title: Common Read 2020-21
content: ## Stories of Courage and Connections

title: Common Read 2020-21
content: ### ![The words "What is your Moth story?" appear over a photo of the Common Read book cover](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/moth_story_banner.gif)
section: ![The words "What is your Moth story?" appear over a photo of the Common Read book cover](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/moth_story_banner.gif)

title: Common Read 2020-21
content:
### About This Project

*The Moth Presents: Occasional Magic* features true personal stories about defying the impossible: “Occasional magic refers to those moments of beauty, wonder, and clarity, often stumbled upon, where we suddenly see a piece of truth about our life.”

Our current social context of a global pandemic and the continued fight against racial injustices challenges us to recognize the "Occasional Magic" of this moment and to show courage in the face of what may seem impossible.

title: Common Read 2020-21
content:
### Prompt

Think about the most important and impactful moments during your time at Cal Poly Pomona:  the moments when you spoke your truth on campus; the moments when you joined others and fought for your values and beliefs; the moments when you learned something new from a faculty member, a student, a facilities worker that changed your perspective; the moments when you helped to create change.

The moments when you created a sense of belonging for and with others; the moments when Cal Poly Pomona embodied its mission of being an “Inclusive PolyTechnic” even if it was for a brief moment.

Tell your own CPP “Moth” story, a true personal story about finding the courage to face what may have seemed impossible and make Cal Poly Pomona better. Creating social change and making the "impossible" possible can be found in moments that are every day, extraordinary, and everything in between. Your story of courage at CPP doesn't have to be epic, but should be true and significant to you.

title: Common Read 2020-21
content:
### Who Can Participate

Anyone in the CPP community of students, faculty, staff and administrators can participate.

All student submissions will receive a copy of the current CPP Common Read book, *The Moth Presents: Occasional Magic* and be one of the first to receive next year’s CPP Common Read book (TBA).

![The words "How to Participate" in front of a photo of the Common Read cover ](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/participate_banner.jpg)

Tell your story using Microsoft Sway, an storytelling tool you can access in Office 365 in [MyCPP](https://my.cpp.edu/). Find how-tos on Sway on LinkedIn Learning in [MyCPP](https://my.cpp.edu/). Submit your Sway link using the form below.

title: Common Read
content:
## Fall 2021 Common Read Event

**November 16 at 4 p.m. PT**

The First Year Experience hosted a virtual discussion with Dr. Jamil Zaki, the author of Cal Poly Pomona’s Common Read selection for 2021-22: *The War for Kindness: Building Empathy in a Fractured World*.

[Watch the Recording (CPP Login Required)](https://streaming.cpp.edu/media/1_k02z84rf "Watch the Recording (CPP Login Required). ")

title: Common Read
content: ## ![Photo of War on Kindness book laying on a background of book spines](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/war-on-kindness-book)
section: ![Photo of War on Kindness book laying on a background of book spines](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/war-on-kindness-book)

title: Common Read
content: ## 2021-22 Common Read

title: Common Read
content:
### The War for Kindness: Building Empathy in a Fractured World

Empathy is in short supply. Isolation and tribalism are rampant. We struggle to understand people who aren’t like us, but find it easy to hate them. Studies show that we are less caring than we were even thirty years ago. In 2006, Barack Obama said that the United States is suffering from an “empathy deficit.” Since then, things only seem to have gotten worse.

It doesn’t have to be this way. In this groundbreaking book, Jamil Zaki shares cutting-edge research, including experiments from his own lab, showing that empathy is not a fixed trait—something we’re born with or not—but rather a skill that can be strengthened through effort. He also tells the stories of people who embody this new perspective, fighting for kindness in the most difficult of circumstances. We meet a former neo-Nazi who is now helping extract people from hate groups, ex-prisoners discussing novels with the judge who sentenced them, Washington police officers changing their culture to decrease violence among their ranks, and NICU nurses fine-tuning their empathy so that they don’t succumb to burnout.

Written with clarity and passion, *The War for Kindness* is an inspiring call to action. The future may depend on whether we accept the challenge.
section: The War for Kindness: Building Empathy in a Fractured World

title: Common Read
content:
### About the Author

Jamil Zaki is a professor of psychology at Stanford University and the director of the Stanford Social Neuroscience Lab. Using tools from psychology and neuroscience, he and his colleagues examine how empathy works and how people can learn to empathize more effectively. His writing on these topics has appeared in the New York Times, the Washington Post, the New Yorker, and the Atlantic. He lives in San Francisco with his wife and their two daughters.

title: Common Read
content:
## Kindness Challenges

To put the book’s principles into practice, Jamil Zaki developed a series of Kindness Challenges. There are 5 challenges you can use in your classroom to see what it’s like to work at being a little kinder.

[See the Challenges](https://www.warforkindness.com/challenges "See the Challenges. ")

![Photo of IGE 1020H course presenting on project ](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/fye-disabilityproject.jpg)

title: Common Read
content:
## FYE Student Project: IGE 1020H

Students in the IGE 1020H course partnered with staff from the Disability Resource Center and Information Technology & Institutional Planning to gather information for accessible pathways on the CPP campus.

“As a class we read the book ‘The War for Kindness’ by Jamil Zaki by exploring our textbooks. The last step was this class project on taking action that can help change our community,” said Assistant Professor Hyeryung Hwang.

[Read More on the Poly Post](https://thepolypost.com/news/2022/05/10/honors-class-maps-out-campus-accessibility/ "Read More on the Poly Post . ")

title: 2022-2023 Common Read
content: # 2022-2023 Common Read

title: 2022-2023 Common Read
content: ## Braiding Sweetgrass Indigenous Wisdom, Scientific Knowledge and the Teachings of Plants by Robin Wall Kimmerer
section: Braiding Sweetgrass Indigenous Wisdom, Scientific Knowledge and the Teachings of Plants by Robin Wall Kimmerer

title: 2022-2023 Common Read
content:
## ![Photo of Braiding Sweetgrass book resting on a background of green grass](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/braiding-sweetgrass.jpg)

As a botanist, Robin Wall Kimmerer has been trained to ask questions of nature with the tools of science. As a member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation, she embraces the notion that plants and animals are our oldest teachers. In Braiding Sweetgrass, Kimmerer brings these two lenses of knowledge together to take us on “a journey that is every bit as mythic as it is scientific, as sacred as it is historical, as clever as it is wise” (Elizabeth Gilbert).

Drawing on her life as an indigenous scientist, a mother, and a woman, Kimmerer shows how other living beings—asters and goldenrod, strawberries and squash, salamanders, algae, and sweetgrass—offer us gifts and lessons, even if we’ve forgotten how to hear their voices. In a rich braid of reflections that range from the creation of Turtle Island to the forces that threaten its flourishing today, she circles toward a central argument: that the awakening of a wider ecological consciousness requires the acknowledgment and celebration of our reciprocal relationship with the rest of the living world. For only when we can hear the languages of other beings will we be capable of understanding the generosity of the earth, and learn to give our own gifts in return.

![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white-mobile.svg)
![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white.svg)

title: 2023-24 Common Read
content: # 2023-24 Common Read

title: 2023-24 Common Read
content: ## The Sum of Us: What Racism Costs Everyone and How We Can Prosper Together
section: The Sum of Us: What Racism Costs Everyone and How We Can Prosper Together

title: 2023-24 Common Read
content:
## ![Photo of the Common Read book for 2023-24](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/commonread-thesumofus-cover-2.jpg)

In The Sum of Us, Heather McGhee dives into her specialty, the American Economy, and the mystery of why it often fails the American public. From the financial crisis to rising student debt to collapsing public infrastructure, McGhee has found a common root problem: racism. McGhee explains that racism has costs for white people too and it is the common denominator of our most vexing public problems, the core dysfunction of our democracy and constitutive of the spiritual and moral crises that grip us all.

McGhee embarks on a deeply personal journey across the country from Mississippi to California to Maine, tallying what we lose when we buy into the zero-sum paradigm--the idea the progress for some of us must come at the expense of others. Along the way, she meets white people who confide in her about losing their homes, their dreams, and their shot at better jobs to the toxic mix of American racism and greed. This is the story of how public goods in this country--from parks and pools to functioning schools--have become private luxuries; of how unions collapsed, wages stagnated, and inequality increased; and how this country, unique among the world's advanced economies, has thwarted universal healthcare.

But in unlikely places of worship and work, McGhee finds proof of what she calls the Solidarity Dividend: gains that come when people come together across race, to accomplish what we simply can't do on our own.

The Sum of Us is a brilliant analysis of how we arrived here: divided and self-destructing, materially rich but spiritually starved and vastly unequal. McGhee marshals economic and socialogical research to paint an irrefutable story of racism's costs, but at the heart of the book are the humble stories of people yearning to be part of a better America, including white supremacy's collateral victims: white people themselves. With startling empathy, this hearfelt message from a Black woman to a multiracial America leaves us with a new vision for a future in which we finally realize that life can be more than zero-sum.

![Heather McGhee speaks at Cal Poly Pomona for common read event](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/common-read-heather-mcghee.jpg)
section: ![Photo of the Common Read book for 2023-24](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/commonread-thesumofus-cover-2.jpg)

title: Common Read
content: ## 2024-25 Common Read

title: Common Read
content:
### Crying in H Mart by Michelle Zauner

In this exquisite story of family, food, grief, and endurance, Michelle Zauner proves herself far more than a dazzling singer, songwriter, and guitarist. With humor and heart, she tells of growing up one of the few Asian American kids at her school in Eugene, Oregon; of struggling with her mother’s particular, high expectations of her; of a painful adolescence; of treasured months spent in her grandmother’s tiny apartment in Seoul, where she and her mother would bond, late at night, over heaping plates of food.
As she grew up, moving to the East Coast for college, finding work in the restaurant industry, and performing gigs with her fledgling band, and meeting the man who would become her husband, her Koreanness began to feel ever more distant, even as she found the life she wanted to live. It was her mother’s diagnosis of terminal cancer, when Michelle was twenty-five, that forced a reckoning with her identity and brought her to reclaim the gifts of taste, language, and history her mother had given her.
Vivacious and plainspoken, lyrical and honest, Zauner’s voice is as radiantly alive on the page as it is onstage. Rich with intimate anecdotes that will resonate widely, and complete with family photos, Crying in H Mart is a book to cherish, share, and reread.

![Jacqueline Naranjo holds Crying in H Mart book](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/img/fye/naranjo-commonread.jpg)

content: ## 2025-26 Common Read

title: Common Read
content:
### I Never Thought of It That Way by Mónica Guzmán

In this thoughtful and practical book, journalist and author Mónica Guzmán provides a practical guide and toolkit to navigate conversations and connect despite differences. With real-world examples and everyday tools, Guzmán encourages readers to listen actively, stay curious, and remain engaged with the world around them, especially when faced with differing perspectives. The book was nominated by Jason Wang, visitor experience counselor in the Office of Outreach, Recruitment and Educational Partnerships, who appreciated how its practical insights support thoughtful conversations. He believes the book offers meaningful support for first-year students as they encounter new perspectives and adjust to college life.

![ason Wang with I Never Thought of it That Way by Monica Guzman, this years Common Read ](../img/common-read-2025.jpg)

![2025-26  Common Read Keynote March 10, 2026 11:45 a.m. to 1 p.m.](../img/guzman-register.png)

title: First Year Experience Course Outcomes
content: # First Year Experience Course Outcomes

title: First Year Experience Course Outcomes
content:
## First Year Student Learning Outcomes

FYE courses at CPP aim to orient students to the university and set them up for college and career success. Courses designated as FYE at CPP are designed to achieve four student learning outcomes (SLOs):

1. Students will use information resources appropriately for professional and/or personal success.
2. Students will engage in campus opportunities for personal development.
3. Students will identify various academic and career paths.
4. Students will engage in a learn-by-doing experience to develop transferrable skills.

title: First Year Experience
content:
## FYE Mission Statement

To support the university’s efforts to increase undergraduate retention and academic success by providing programs that facilitate the students’ transition to the university through experiences that outline the university’s academic expectations, support services, social resources and opportunities.

![Ethan Lamas talks about his research as part of the First Year Experience Showcase ](img/fye-mission-image.png)

title: First Year Experience
content:
## First Year Experience Program

Learn how to be successful in your first-year at Cal Poly Pomona. The first-year experience course provides an overview of the opportunities and possible careers associated with your major. Earn college credit by enrolling in a first-year experience this fall. As part of the program, students engage in activities, community service projects, field trips, and innovative learning opportunities....

title: FYE PolyX Showcase
content:
## Your First Step in Cal Poly Pomona’s Learn-by-Doing Journey

First-year students participate in a Signature Polytechnic Experience (PolyX) within their FYE courses and present their work at the PolyX Showcase. This early immersion in Cal Poly Pomona's become-by-doing approach and polytechnic advantage prepares students for success from their first semester.

title: Faculty and Staff Resources
content:
### FYE Learning Committee Information

×

The FYE Learning Community brings together faculty and staff interested in supporting first-year students through collaborative professional development. Participants will create or refine FYE course modules aligned with student learning outcomes, share best practices, and receive guidance from the CAFE team.

**Who should apply:** Faculty (tenure-track and adjunct) and staff working with first-year students.
 **Meeting Format:** In person, Thursdays from 11:45 a.m. to 1:00 p.m.
 **Meeting Dates:**

* September: 9 & 26
* October: 9 & 23
* November 6 & 20
* December 4

**Stipend:** $500 for eligible faculty upon completion

**Facilitators:** Dr. Nina Abramzon, Dr. Dora Lee, and Dr. Donya Rahimi

[Apply Now!](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=HqZLFuw5XU-J_6ofAKUhtKHzEZbWxoRClS96C7xkreJUNjRHN1BUVEE3VlFZNVQyQ0RKVVJMV05OSC4u&route=shorturl)

title: Graduation Initiative 2025
content: # Graduation Initiative 2025

title: Graduation Initiative 2025
content:
## About GI 2025

​In an effort to better prepare and assist students, the CSU has established the Graduation Initiative 2025 to remove obstacles to receiving a baccalaureate degree.

GI 2025 will increase graduation rates for 475,000 students across all 23 CSU campuses.

To meet the workforce demands of California’s innovation economy in the years and decades to come, GI 2025 will add 100,000 more baccalaureate degree-educated citizens to California over the next 10 years.

This would bring the total number of expected CSU graduates between 2015 and 2025 alone to more than 1 million.

# Graduation Pledge Programs at Cal Poly Pomona

![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white-mobile.svg)

section: Graduation Pledge Programs at Cal Poly Pomona

title: Graduation Pledge Programs at Cal Poly Pomona
content:
## About the Program

The Graduation Pledge programs at Cal Poly Pomona can help you stay on track towards a timely graduation. The programs support incoming students in earning a bachelor's degree within two (for transfer students) or four years (for freshmen).

![Graduate cheering with diploma in hand.](img/grad-home-feature.png)

title: Graduation Pledge Programs at Cal Poly Pomona
content:
## Participating in the Program

CPP offers Through in Two (for transfer students) and Finish in Four (for first-time freshmen) as our graduation pledge program, which is [a legislatively-mandated program](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202320240AB1205). Students who are eligible for the program promise to balance school, work and personal responsibilities and stay committed to their education. CPP will provide an effective and coordinated advising program to help students register for the appropriate classes and stay on track to graduate. Graduation Pledge students receive priority registration appointments for the fall, winter, spring and summer terms.

The program does not provide tuition assistance or tuition reimbursement.

title: Graduation Pledge Programs at Cal Poly Pomona
content:
## Eligibility and Next Steps

Incoming students who are eligible for the program will receive an invitation to apply. Learn more about our [2-year](2-year-info.shtml) and [4-year](4-year-info.shtml) programs.

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content: # Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
section: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
## About the 2-Year Program

![Students at College of Environmental Design Commencement look off camera](img/2-year.jpg)

This 2-year program enables the California State University, including Cal Poly Pomona, to establish programs for students who are pursuing an Associate Degree for Transfer (ADT) from any California Community College and plan to complete their bachelor's degrees in two years at a CSU campus. This legislatively-mandated program is limited to students who are California residents.

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
## Eligibility Requirements

To be eligible, you must:

* Be a California Resident
* Enter fall term with a completed Associate Degree for Transfer (ADT), Associate in Arts for Transfer (AA-T), or Associate in Science for Transfer (AS-T) in a major that is deemed similar at CPP. You can find the list of similar majors and options on the [CSU website](https://www.calstate.edu/apply/transfer/Pages/associate-degree-for-transfer-major-and-campus-search.aspx).
* Your ADT must be completed in the Spring term prior to Fall admission term.

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Monitor Your Email and BroncoDirect To Do List

If you are eligible for the 2-year program, you will receive an invitation to apply. This will be your only chance during your time at Cal Poly Pomona to apply.

You will receive a confirmation email in your CPP inbox when we receive your application. If you're accepted, you'll receive a second email admitting you into the program.

If you're not eligible for the program or miss the application period, don't worry! You will still have access to your assigned academic advisor, and you can still graduate within two years by planning for 30 semester units per year. Visit the Take 30 website to learn more about the benefits.
section: Monitor Your Email and BroncoDirect To Do List

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Apply for the Program by the Deadline

Apply by **August 18, 2025**.
section: Apply for the Program by the Deadline

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Enroll in 15 Units Your First Term

You will register for classes at your mandatory Orientation program. You will also be able to adjust your schedule during your Orientation course registration window and Add/Drop Period.
section: Enroll in 15 Units Your First Term

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Update Your Academic Planner With Your 2025-26 Courses

Use [Academic Planner](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/advising/planner.shtml) to map out your Fall 2025 and Spring 2026 courses.
section: Update Your Academic Planner With Your 2025-26 Courses

title: Through in Two: 2-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Submit Final Transcript with ADT Awarded

Your AS-T or AA-T must be similiar to a CPP major. Your AS-T or AA-T must be awarded in the spring prior to the fall admit term.

Please note that your acceptance is conditional until we confirm your ADT status. If your self-reported ADT is not verified by the Office of Admissions, you will not be eligible to continue in the program in the second year. We will send you more communication about this process.
section: Submit Final Transcript with ADT Awarded

title: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content: # Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
section: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program

title: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
## About the 4-Year Program

![two students at Commencement put up peace signs](img/4-year.jpg)

This 4-year program enables the California State University, including Cal Poly Pomona, to establish to establish pledge programs for entering first-time students who are both interested and able to complete a bachelor's degree in four years. This legislatively-mandated program is limited to students who are California residents and meet specific criteria.

title: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
## Eligibility

You must be a California resident and a first-year student entering fall term. You must also meet one of the following eligibility requirements:

* Be a first-generation student
* Was eligible for the [Cal State Apply application waiver](https://www.calstate.edu/apply/paying-for-college/pages/fee-waiver.aspx) when you applied to Cal Poly Pomona
* [Be from a high school](https://www.cde.ca.gov/ds/ad/filessp.asp) located in a community with a population of students that receive free and reduced lunch at a rate of 66% and higher

title: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Monitor Your Email and BroncoDirect To Do List

If you are eligible for the 4-year, you will receive an invitation to apply. This will be your only chance during your time at Cal Poly Pomona to apply.

You will receive a confirmation email in your CPP inbox when we receive your application. If you're accepted, you'll receive a second email admitting you into the program.

If you're not eligible for the program or miss the application period, don't worry! You will still have access to your assigned academic advisor, and you can still graduate within two years by planning for 30 semester units per year. Visit the Take 30 website to learn more about the benefits.
section: Monitor Your Email and BroncoDirect To Do List

title: Finish in Four: 4-Year Graduation Pledge Program
content:
### Apply for the Program by the Deadline

Apply by **August 18, 2025**.
section: Apply for the Program by the Deadline

You will register for classes at your mandatory Orientation program. You will also be able to adjust your schedule during your Orientation course registration window and Add/Drop Period.

title: FAQs
content: ## About Our Programs

title: FAQs
content:
### Who can join the program, and how do I know if I'm eligible?

Incoming freshmen and transfer students are eligible to join the program during the fall term by invitation only.
section: Who can join the program, and how do I know if I'm eligible?

title: FAQs
content:
### If I change my option within my declared major, can I remain in the program?

Yes. You can change your option within your declared major and still remain in the program.
section: If I change my option within my declared major, can I remain in the program?

title: FAQs
content:
### Can I add a minor and still be eligible?

You can add a minor and still be eligible for the program. You should declare it as soon as possible within your four-year or two-year plan, and you must complete it by the end of your plan.
section: Can I add a minor and still be eligible?

title: FAQs
content:
### Can I add a second major and still be eligible?

You can't double major in the program.
section: Can I add a second major and still be eligible?

title: FAQs
content:
### If I'm not eligible to apply for the program this fall, can I reapply next fall?

You can only apply to the  program during your first term at Cal Poly Pomona.
section: If I'm not eligible to apply for the program this fall, can I reapply next fall?

title: FAQs
content:
### If I'm not eligible for the program, can I still graduate in two or four years?

If you are not eligible for the program, you can still graduate in two or four years. Your academic advisor can help you strategize a plan. You can also visit [Student Success Central](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/index.shtml) to find academic and advising resources, support directories, self-services and more that will help you stay on track to graduation. You should also plan to take 30 semester units per year to stay on track to graduate. Visit the [Take 30 website](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/take30/index.shtml) to learn more about the benefits.
section: If I'm not eligible for the program, can I still graduate in two or four years?

title: FAQs
content:
### For this program, how many units should I complete per year?

During your first term, you must enroll in and maintain 15 units at Cal Poly Pomona. To maintain eligibility in the program, you must successfully complete 30 semester units toward your degree by the end of summer term every year.
section: For this program, how many units should I complete per year?

title: FAQs
content:
### Can my test credit/transfer coursework count toward the 30-unit requirement?

* For first-time freshmen:
  + In the first year, a student’s AP and transfer credit can be applied to their 30-unit total as long as they enroll in 15 units their first term.
  + In subsequent years, students may consider taking transfer credit at a community college during the summer to apply to their 30 units each academic year.
* For transfer students:
  + Students may consider taking transfer credit at a community college during the summer to apply to their 30 units each academic year.
section: Can my test credit/transfer coursework count toward the 30-unit requirement?

title: FAQs
content:
### How do I receive advising once I am accepted into the program?

Your first advising session will be at Orientation. After you are accepted to a program, your academic advisor will help you plan your classes and stay on track to graduation.
section: How do I receive advising once I am accepted into the program?

title: FAQs
content:
### When do I receive priority registration?

You will have already registered for your first courses at Orientation by the application deadline. Once you're in the program, your first term of priority registration will be the following spring semester.
section: When do I receive priority registration?

title: FAQs
content:
### Should I fill out the application before or after Orientation?

You can apply anytime before the deadline. We recommend that you attend your Orientation date before you begin the application process so you know what courses you are taking in fall.
section: Should I fill out the application before or after Orientation?

title: FAQs
content:
### What's the difference with priority registration through Graduation Pledge and other programs?

There is no difference – all programs that offer priority registration have the same priority registration dates. However, each program has specific eligibility requirements that may impact your priority registration privilege if you do not meet them. As long as you maintain eligibility in the program, you will receive priority registration.
section: What's the difference with priority registration through Graduation Pledge and other programs?

title: FAQs
content:
### Does the program get full?

We do not have a limit on how many students can apply for and stay in the program.

title: FAQs
content:
### What would happen if I fail a class?

Receiving less than a C- letter grade or a No Credit will make you ineligible for the program. There is no grace or probation period.
section: What would happen if I fail a class?

title: Maintaining Eligibility
content: # Maintaining Eligibility

title: Maintaining Eligibility
content:
## Requirements to Maintain Eligibility for Continuing Students

**To maintain eligibility you must:**

* Monitor CPP email with program deadlines, registration, upcoming workshops, etc.
* Remain in the same major you selected when you came to CPP (unless Undeclared).
* Successfully complete 30 units toward your degree by the end of summer term every academic year.
* Complete GE Areas A2/1A (English) and B4/2 (Math) by summer term of your first year (if you're a part of the 4-Year program).
* Maintain a minimum grade point average of 2.5.
* Earn a C- or better or CR (Credit) in all course work taken.
* Be continuously enrolled for the fall and spring terms each year.
* Submit your academic plan for priority registration by the deadline each term. Create an appointment via [CPP Connect](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/cppconnect/index.shtml "https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/cppconnect/index.shtml") with your [advisor](http://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/directories/advising-directory.shtml "http://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/directories/advising-directory.shtml") if you need assistance updating your academic plan.
* Enroll in at least 15 units at Cal Poly Pomona in your first fall term.
section: Requirements to Maintain Eligibility for Continuing Students

title: I Am First
content:
## Our Goals

1. Create a visibility campaign of first-generation students, faculty, and staff community at Cal Poly Pomona
2. Create access and equity for First Generation College Students (FGCS) through faculty/staff mentorships

title: I Am First
content:
## At Cal Poly Pomona, you are a first-generation student if you are any of the following:

1. A student with neither of their natural or adoptive parents received a baccalaureate degree
2. A student who, prior to the age of 18, regularly resided with and received support from only one parent and whose supporting parent did not receive a baccalaureate degree
3. An individual who, prior to the age of 18, did not reside with or receive support from a natural or adoptive parent
section: At Cal Poly Pomona, you are a first-generation student if you are any of the following:

title: I Am First
content:
## How do we support our students?

The transition to college is challenging but it can be even more difficult for those who do not have a parent or guardian who is familiar with the college enrollment process and the expectations of campus life. We have a responsibility to help first-generation college students to navigate the university and help them translate the important life skills they bring with them that will support their academic success.

**We recognize that first-generation students bring with them a wealth of strengths and skills to Cal Poly Pomona, including:**

* Aspirations
* Diversity
* Linguistic Abilities/Bilingualism
* Persistence
* Resilience

![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white-mobile.svg)
![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white.svg)
section: How do we support our students?

title: I Am First
content:
## Cal Poly Pomona is a First-Gen Forward Campus

This designation from the Center for First-generation Student Success, an initiative of the National Association of Student Personnel Administrators (NASPA), recognizes institutions of higher education that have demonstrated a commitment to improving experiences and advancing outcomes of first-generation college students. CPP was included in the 2021 cohort of First-Gen Forward colleges and universities.

[Learn more on PolyCentric!](https://www.cpp.edu/news/content/2021/07/cal-poly-pomona-receives-first-gen-forward-designation-for-commitment-to-student-success/index.shtml%20)

![Faculty and Staff holding I Am First flags and](img/firstfoward.png)

![Student holding I Am First Shirt](img/firstkickoff.png)
section: Cal Poly Pomona is a First-Gen Forward Campus

title: I Am First
content:
## CPP's I Am First Campaign Wins Telly Award

Cal Poly Pomona's "I Am First" Campaign, led by the Office of Student Success, Equity, and Innovation, recently earned a Telly Award, specifically a bronze in the educational institutional non-broadcast category. This initiative supports first-generation college students, recognizing the unique challenges they face, including navigating the enrollment process without familial...

[Read more on PolyCentric](https://www.cpp.edu/news/content/2023/11/cal-poly-pomona-i-am-first-campaign-wins-telly-award/index.shtml%20)

title: Mentorship Program
content:
## About Our Mentorship Program

In the I Am First mentoring program, CPP first-generation college students are matched with a faculty or staff member who also identifies as a first-generation college graduate. The shared experiences of being first-generation foster a sense of belonging and create a pathway to success for CPP students.

![Sara Garcia (left), a nutrition student, and Ariana Estrada (right), a scholarship coordinator with Financial Aid and Scholarships, were matched through the I AM FIRST mentorship program in 2018.](img/i-am-first3.jpg)

title: Mentorship Program
content:

[### I Am First Mentor Handbook](i-am-first-mentor-handbook.shtml "opens a new window")

title: Mentorship Program
content:
### The Mentor's Impact

Mentors help students transition into Cal Poly Pomona while guiding them in academic and career planning. They make a meaningful impact by connecting students to the university and shaping future leaders through support and encouragement.

title: Mentorship Program
content:
### Mentee Benefits

Mentees gain one-on-one mentoring, building lasting relationships with faculty and staff. They receive specialized support and resources to help them succeed academically and personally.

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## Get to know your I Am First community

Many CPP faculty and staff self-identify as among the first in their families to go to college or be the first to graduate. Click on the profiles of CPP faculty and staff below to learn more about their experiences as first-generation college students.
section: Get to know your I Am First community

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## First-Gen Journeys | Our Faculty & Staff Share Their Stories

[![Maria Anzaldo](../img/anzaldo.jpg)
section: First-Gen Journeys | Our Faculty & Staff Share Their Stories

content:

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

They matter and have a contribution to make to anywhere they go in life. They should ask questions and participate in events and campus experiences that will contribute to their growth and development. If they have a goal of going to graduate school, they should do it. Find someone on-campus that will guide and support you with your post-graduation plans.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

My college experience as a first-generation college student was overwhelming. I couldn't go to my parents because they didn't understand the commitment required, financial aid jargon, and the need to spend hours studying. I had to adjust my study habits based on what I had going on in my personal life and learn how to navigate college on my own and with the help of friends. My college experience made me realize what I wanted to do after graduation. I decided I wanted to be a role model and mentor for other students to help them achieve their own educational goals.

Close

[![Diana Ascencio](../img/ascenio-2.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
### Diana Ascencio

#### Advising Center Coordinator, College of Science Advising Center

B.S. in Business Administration, Cal Poly Pomona

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Get involved! Take advantage of all the resources available through student advocacy and programming. Ask questions and be critical of information that they may receive. Most importantly try new things, and step out of comfort to gain better understanding of themselves and the world around them.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I did not really understand the academic institution/system. I took classes not needed for my degree and did not understand the requirements or importance of attending office hours and developing relationships with faculty. I also struggled balancing familial demands, school, understanding myself, my needs, and time management overall. Although I had an older brother at CPP with me, we were both just as lost. We had to figure it out on our own without help from our parents because they were not familiar with how to navigate through the institution.

As an undergraduate student I became heavily involved through my years with the [Cultural Centers](https://www.cpp.edu/~oslcc/), the undocumented club on campus, the Multicultural Council, [ASI Student Government](https://asi.cpp.edu/student-government/), [Orientation Services](https://www.cpp.edu/~orientation/index.shtml),[PolyTransfer](https://www.cpp.edu/~polytransfer/), and the[Undocumented Services](https://www.cpp.edu/~deanofstudents/bdrc/) on campus. Being involved helped me find resources and support from faculty and staff to reach beyond my own limits. If I had not tapped into my community for help in navigating through the academic institution, I would not be where I am today.

Close

[![Brandi Blair](../img/blair.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

content:

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Give yourself time to learn who you are. Set high, but attainable standards, and measure your success by those standards. Allow yourself the flexibility to grow and change and want new things in your life. Make temporary sacrifices when necessary for long-term success. You never know what the path you are paving today will lead to, so say yes to every opportunity, and never regret an experience.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I feel a lot of pressure in my role as a first-generation college student to excel in life and lead by example. My family couldn't help me and my parents were not at all involved in the process of applying for schools or choosing my major. I enjoyed college but I had to rely on myself to set my goals and stay motivated to achieve them. I utilized resources at school such as the career center, [federal work study](https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/federal-work-study/index.shtml), and teachers' office hours in order to excel. I experienced so much satisfaction at graduation, knowing I had invested six years of my life in pursuit of that moment.

Close

[![Won Choi](../img/choi.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Get connected with people who achieved goals that are similar to yours. Learn from their experience. Get their advice. Get recommendations and referrals to resources.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I worked hard and did well academically, but nobody was available in my personal life to offer advice on career options, resources, and opportunities. I would've done more in college had I known what resources and options were available. My career path may be different had I known what career options were available. Eventually I learned earning good grades is not enough. Academic success must be supplemented by meeting the right people combined with involvement in the right activities.

Close

[![Wendy Córdova](../img/cordova.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

First Generation is an identity. We are the first in our family to embark on something never done before. Be proud to be on this journey and along the way you will find that there are others just like us who will support you. Find yourself a mentor and/or fementor!
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I was seventeen when I moved way to go to college at UC Santa Barbara: three hours away from my family. When times were difficult I told myself, "I belong here. You earned this!" When times were good I was often surrounded by friends who are now chosen family.

Close

[![Berlinda Crawford](../img/crawford1.jpg)

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Three things: 1. You're not alone, 2. It's ok to ask for help, and 3. Learn from your mistakes (because they will happen).
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I was always afraid that others would see me as not good enough for college, so I tried my best to blend in and not draw attention. I wouldn't raise my hand in class, wouldn't volunteer for projects, and wouldn't talk to my professors. When I started to struggle I especially wouldn't seek help, but instead tried fixing things on my own, which usually didn't work well. The reality was that I had a lot of support on campus, I just needed to accept the help.

Close

[![Dr. Amy Dao](../img/dao.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

content:
### Amy Dao

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Be open and take advantage of all of the resources at your fingertips--human, monetary, opportunity. Never let your ego get in the way of asking for help or advice. You don't know what you don't know until you know it! Remember where you came from and give back when you have the capability.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

It was one of the best and most life changing experiences. I would never have been an academic without the love and support from my faculty mentors--who are still a part of my life today. I was also very much involved in university life so I was never bored.

Close

[![Ariana Estrada](../img/estrada.jpg)

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Welcome to Cal Poly Pomona! We are here to support you and provide guidance throughout your journey in higher education. There are staff, advisors, and faculty that are willing to assist in providing services, resources, and mentorship which can foster a sense of belonging. Be sure to get involved with campus organizations, groups, and affiliations as this may lead to long lasting connections and friendships. Most importantly: ask questions. Attending a university may be unfamiliar to you, however, every question asked may provide greater clarity to ensure that you are on the correct path. We look forward to watching you succeed!
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

As a first-generation college student, I had to familiarize myself with all aspects of higher education ranging from courses, resources to services. Connections made with peers, advisors and faculty made the transition much easier and provided many opportunities. Many of the connections were made as I became actively involved on campus, participated in classroom discussions, and asked questions. My mentors, staff, and classmates made graduation possible. The highlight of my educational experience was participating in the graduation ceremony with my family. I look forward to meeting you all and assisting you throughout this new journey!

Close

[![Dr. Jack Fong](../img/fong.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

To always observe those who succeed and learn from them. Also, to never give up.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

College was culture shock. Although I grew up in America, the culture of academia was a place that I initially felt out of place at. Over time, after getting my BA, MA, and PhD at a variety of different institutions, I still ended up feeling this way. This is because I left my geographical womb of Southeast Asia, and much of that region's heritage is still very much within me.

Close

[![Amber Freeland](../img/freeland.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**
