title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Seek out help from other first-generation leaders. Continue to pursue your dreams in the face of adversity--not everything will come easy and many times there will be road blocks that seem insurmountable but they are not. Maintain your tenacity and fulfill your goals. All of the hard work will pay off.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

It was difficult navigating college--setting up financial aid, figuring out all of the policies and procedures, and locating resources for help and guidance. It was also very rewarding. I had many opportunities that I would not have had if I did not attend college, such as working on the world renowned [Rose Float](https://www.cpp.edu/~rosefloatlab/), working internships that guided me toward my career path, and learning valuable business knowledge and strategies I still use today. Striking a balance between work, studying, internships, and extracurriculars was challenging but a valuable lesson. I worked hard and graduated summa cum laude as an undergrad and was the first in my family to obtain a master's degree.

Close

[![Dr. Claudia Garcia-Des Lauriers](../img/garcia.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

content:

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Work hard, be intellectually curious, be kind, be tough, don't take "NO" for an answer, and most importantly be YOU.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I grew up in a very small town so going to college meant going to the "big" city. However, I moved into the dorms, worked hard, applied for every scholarship I could, and poured myself into the activities and opportunities available on campus. By doing this I learned not only the content offered in my classes, but the institution of a university. I was also fortunate to have great mentors from the honors program, faculty mentors, administrative staff, residence staff, colleagues and friends that helped me learn all of the things I did not know. I asked a lot of questions and took every opportunity to learn something new. The key is to find mentors, no matter who they are, and ask lots of questions.

Close

[![Dr. Carol Gonzales](../img/gonzales.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

content:
### Dr. Carol Gonzales

#### Associate Vice President, IT Security and Compliance Chief Information Security Officer

M.S. in Business Administration, Cal Poly Pomona
B.S. in Computer Science, Cal Poly Pomona

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Don't be afraid, intimidated or too proud to reach out and enjoy everything that CPP has for you. CPP is a great and beautiful campus. It offers a lot of services and opportunities to enjoy activities. When something gets in the way, don't wait until the question or concern is "big." The sooner that you deal with it, the sooner it is out of your way so you can move on to the other stuff you want to enjoy and need to work on. Know that we are all hear to help and support you. CPP is a very caring community.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

College opened up my world to so much joy and excitement away from a more stressful world at home. CPP not only helped me grow academically but it helped me develop myself socially and emotionally. I didn't know there was anything called "first-generation." To some degree I felt shame/embarrassment for the opportunity for going to college. So I didn't talk to many people from my old world outside of CPP. There was no one outside of CPP who understood the work or the stress. My classmates and the people of the CPP community understood. I could rely on them.

Close

[![Dr. Mario Guerrero](../img/guerrero.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
### Dr. Mario Guerrero

#### Chair and Associate Professor, Political Science

Click here to meet Dr. Guerrero!](#modal-12 "Meet Dr. Guerrero!")

content:
### Meet Dr. Guerrero!

×

Chair and Associate Professor, Political Science

Ph.D. in Political Science, UC Santa Barbara
M.A. in Political Science, UC Santa Barbara
B.A. in Political Science, UCLA

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

The sky is the limit. While college is a new experience that can sometimes bring what seems like insurmountable challenges, it is an experience that will open up doors. At Cal Poly Pomona, you have access to scholars and faculty who were also the first in their family to go to college. Being here on campus is a powerful experience. Explore it, embrace it, and enjoy it!
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

My parents came to the United States from Mexico in the 1970s in search of the American Dream and a better life for their family. Years later, they couldn't have envisioned their dream manifest itself in a son who would go on to earn a doctoral degree and become a college professor. Although I owe every bit of success to my parents, the road getting here was incredibly challenging, especially when I first got to college. I felt college wasn't made for me. However, after slowly meeting mentors on campus, I persevered. I realized that I needed to invest in my education not only for myself but for people like who my parents who sacrificed so much to get me to that point.

Close

[![Patricia Higuchi](../img/higuchi.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
### Patricia Higuchi

#### Director, Student Accounting and Cashiering Services

Click here to meet Patricia!](#modal-13 "Meet Patricia!")

B.S. in Child Development, Cal State Los Angeles

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?

If you need help, ask for it and don't panic. Someone is always here to help.
section: What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## What was your experience as a first-generation college student?

It was a little bit scary. Your parents don't really know or understand some of the obstacles so you end up trying to do more on your own than is probably necessary. I didn't use the resources that were available to me since I was insecure about them, and worried that I would look "dumb." Well, I was. I should have asked more questions and used the resources available. If I could do it over, I would be more assertive and take advantage of what's available.

Close

[![Linda Hoang](../img/hoang.jpg)
section: What was your experience as a first-generation college student?

content:

content:

×

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

I encourage students to get involved on campus. Make connections with your peers and faculty. If you have the opportunity, join clubs and apply to internships. College can be a lonely experience if you're navigating the system by yourself. Expand your network and ask for help. There are many other students who have gone through similar experiences, and there are people who will be eager to help you.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

As an undergrad student, I balanced work, school, and student leadership positions. My most meaningful experience was working on campus at an advising center. The position prepared me for work after college and the expectations that should be set between supervisor and employee. I was fortunate to have a supervisor who supported me throughout my undergrad experience and set me on the path to working in higher education.

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Ask questions! If you do not understand how to do something, ask someone. Get to know your professors and fellow students. Learn from their knowledge and experiences. Most importantly, know that the faculty and staff of Cal Poly Pomona are here to help you. We want you to succeed and have a positive college experience. Also, take advantage of the various clubs and programs that Cal Poly Pomona offers. Whether they are specific to your major or geared towards your interests, they can help you get to know your peers and allow you to grow as a student and as a person.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

When I started college at Cal Poly Pomona, I felt alone. My friends from high school went to other colleges, so I didn't know anyone on campus. My first year was basically going to class and then going home to study. It wasn't until the middle of my second year that my college experience changed. I joined a couple of clubs on campus and began to meet other people with the same interests. Being involved in campus life and the students I met encouraged, supported, and enhanced my college experience. I didn't feel alone anymore. College isn't just about academics, it's also about meeting new people, learning from their experiences, and becoming a better person.

Close

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

My parents always encouraged me to go beyond high school but could offer me little insight into college success. I often sat in classes silent, paralyzed and unable to utter answers to questions posed for fear of my ignorance being revealed. After my first year, I seriously considered dropping out because of feelings of inadequacy and cultural alienation. I was one of a handful of African American students. But I learned the adaptive skills--I mimicked more successful students and their study patterns. I worked collaboratively with others who were better prepared. I sought out faculty mentors that saw my potential and worked tirelessly with me. I wrote down prepared questions and answers to limit my anxiety in class discussions. I began to seek out courses of study that gave me a sense of identity and purpose. I was empowered to learn that people who came from similar backgrounds were often behind in academic skills because they were born into situations of impoverished schools and neighborhoods. Moreover, these gaps in performance came as a consequence of historical trends and discriminatory policies that limited their options and often diminished their dreams. By my junior and senior years, the students who refused to work with me because of my perceived inferiority, now courted by opinion and sought me out as a study partner. Eventually, I graduated with honors. It was a long path with many obstacles but I have reached the point where I can now help others avoid the many pitfalls of being a first-generation college student.

Close

[![Thavery Lay](../img/thavery.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

You are not alone. College is new and can sometimes be overwhelming; if you don’t know something, it's okay to ask questions. Try new things such as meeting others from different backgrounds or join various clubs and organizations. Visiting your professors during office hours is a must -- this helps them to get to know you better. Apply for scholarships; there are so many funds that go unclaimed. Look for opportunities to work on campus because it is convenient and offices will work around your student schedule. Find opportunties to go study abroad as it will help you see the world and broaden your horizons.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

It was scary for me because I felt intimated. I was afraid I would get lost, wouldn't make any friends, or other students wouldn't be friendly with me. I didn't do as well my first year because I did not have good study habits, although I had always gotten high grades from K-12th grade. College work was different and much more demanding than I was used to. However, I learned to “swim” and got connected with resources and services on campus. Once I found my community and support system, I felt like I belonged.

Close

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

1. Have an academic advisor review your education plan to ensure that you're on track.
2. Look into any and all grants and scholarships to assist you in paying for your tuition if applicable.
3. Get involved on campus and get to know your peers by joining campus clubs and activities.
4. Don't be afraid to ask questions.
5. Establish a great rapport with your university professors.
6. Understand deadlines and special dates regarding registration.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

As a first-generation college student, I had a great experience at Cal Poly Pomona. I was born in Vietnam and the third child of four. I was extremely excited to be the first person in my family to attend a university and ultimately graduated within four years. I had drive because I wanted more for myself and I knew that it would be possible and I wanted to make my parents proud. I had wonderful conversations and office hour visits with my professors who steered me in the right direction and gave me the added support to keep me going when times were rough. Although I didn't get involved on campus because I had a full class load and a 32 hours peer week work schedule, I always wished I had so that I was able to experience all the benefits of attending a university.

Close

It's important to build a community around you that can help your success. I would not have made it through college without the support of my family, faculty, and staff. They not only supported me in time of need but they challenged me and did not allow me to give up.

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

My college experience as a first-generation student was filled with difficulties and success. It was hard to get used to being away from home and living with roommates. I also had trouble with time management and studying for exams. Thankfully, I was part of a first-generation program that helped me and, by the second quarter, I improved in those areas and got involved in a club on campus. This club changed my life and I made friends that I still have to this present day. Without the proper support and intentionally community building I might not have made it through college. I'm forever thankful to those who took time to help and support me.

Close

My family was supportive, but didn’t necessarily understand what I was going through during college. Making connections on campus helped me overcome these challenges. I was able to find mentors that validated my experience and supported me through anything I endured.

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Don't give up. Find the drive that can motivate you when you want to give up. It is too easy to walk away from challenges, but the reward you get when you overcome them will stay with you forever. Be open to change and to the opportunities that cross your path. We not only learn and benefit from our successes, but from our failures as well. Ask for help (and keep asking). There are many resources on campus available to you and they can make a huge difference in how you accomplish your goals. Get involved. Being connected to your campus, solidifies your sense of belonging and worthiness of being here.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

Though I had tremendous support and encouragement from my parents, I took full responsibility for my education. I got involved right away with [Rose Float](https://www.cpp.edu/~rosefloatlab/). I inadvertently created another support network for myself, along with establishing many life-long relationships. Upperclassmen provided advice and were examples of perseverance for me. I got married at the end of my second year and soon became a mother. After the births of my children, I could not be involved on campus as deeply as I had been or take on as many classes, but I gained a new determination. Finishing my degree ensured my ability to support my family and continuing my education fulfilled my calling in helping others on their paths to accomplishing their dreams.

Close

It was difficult. I didn't feel like I belonged in college. I enjoyed the learning aspects but I didn't know how to approach my professors, or even if I should. I also quickly realized there were massive gaps in my knowledge of higher education. It seemed that many of my classmates already knew so much because their parents had attended college. I didn't even know the right questions to ask.

Believe that you can accomplish even your biggest, most wishful goals. Almost nothing is as difficult as you expected it to be before you got started, and there is no such thing as a shortcut.

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

When I got to CPP as a first-year student, I felt lost. It seemed like everyone expected me to know about processes and policies that I had never heard of, and I was shy about asking for help because I felt like the only person who didn't know what was going on. One by one, I started to find the people who recognized my value and didn't make me feel self-conscious, and it took me a long time to figure out that a ton of my fellow students felt exactly the same way I felt. I came to CPP when I was 18, and I finished my degree when I was nearly 30. I never would have finished if I didn't start asking for help and learning more about the resources available to me.

Close

content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

I am an only child and both of my parents were against me going to college. I was able to let my anger work for me and achieved my goal to earn both of my college degrees. Be proud of what you are achieving. Never let anyone tell you that it is not necessary to have a college degree, especially if you are a woman! Sometimes the going will get rough, but never give up your dream. You will achieve your goal if you keep forging ahead.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

As a late bloomer, going back to school had always been a dream for me. When I went back to college, because of my age, I assumed that the other younger students would ostracize me in class. I found out just the opposite! The younger students gravitated towards me for direction in our group projects. I considered this quite an honor. In addition, I was able to receive help from some of the younger students in my math courses. Math has always been difficult for me. With my tutors, I was able to excel, which is something I will always cherish.

Close

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

There's a lot more to a university education than what you learn in class. Learning how to learn new material, research what is known about something and synthesize that information into a coherent description, and communicate that synthesis to others, are the most valuable skills. Whatever your major, look for ways that your classroom experiences help you to be better at it. Learn how to manage your time effectively. Most of all, learn what it means to understand a concept deeply enough that you can apply your knowledge in totally new circumstances.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I flunked out after my sophomore year due to family challenges. It took a lot of time and hard work recovering, both financially and academically. I did a lot of jobs I don't want to do again, but doing so made it possible for me to support myself to finish my degree here at Cal Poly Pomona. After that, I have had a wonderful career in graduate school, the aerospace industry, as research staff at UCLA, and now as a faculty member.

Close

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Keep in mind that you are a pioneer, exploring an experience new to you and those around you, you may feel you're on your own. There will be times when you will not know where you'll find the money, or how your next step will play out, but keep going & climbing anyway! It took me five years to graduate with my Bachelor's, it took me nine years to complete my graduate degrees. At the time I felt I was behind. Now I recognize I was on my own timeline, just right for my priorities.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I am the daughter of immigrants from Mexico. My father was a farmworker. He brought us to the US when I was 11 years old. My parents completed elementary and middle school education. They always emphasized education and hard work, and encouraged me to aim for college, but as their first daughter, when it was my time to apply, they said no to applying to colleges away from home. I applied anyway. I was so excited when my acceptance letter came in the mail, but also scared that now I had to share the news. After many, many, many conversations my parents eventually said yes. I was admitted to [EOP](https://www.cpp.edu/ssep/eop/) and [Summer Bridge](https://www.cpp.edu/ssep/summer-bridge/index.shtml), and those programs provided me with the support I needed. My strategy to choosing my major was to choose my favorite subject in high school. That tells you how unfamiliar I was with the whole process. After receiving guidance from my EOP advisors, I became Undeclared. Initially it felt like a step back, but it was the perfect major for me. It gave me time to make the choice that was right for me. I didn't have much to go on, so I took the Career Exploration class and found my love for Psychology. This led me to pursue graduate degrees with the goal of  becoming a counselor/Psychologist at a University.

Close

[![Sally Romero](../img/romero.jpg)

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Network and connect. I know, it might feel scary or pointless, or you might feel like you don't have time or energy, but Cal Poly Pomona has many resources and services available to you. The moment you network and connect with something/someone on campus you will truly feel how much you belong here. Everyone on campus is here to help and see you succeed. Please feel free to stop by and visit me at the University Library-you are always welcomed!
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

As a first-generation college student I felt very alone navigating the system all by myself. My parents had no clue about registration dates, add/drop, wait lists, parking permits, school fees & tuition, prerequisites, course reserves, internship requirements, submitting proper documentation for graduation before deadline, or ordering/buying regalia. I had the fake-it-til-you-make-it attitude. I did not realize all of the services that were available, because I had no one to tell me. My first couple of years, I never visited my advisor nor visited my professor's office hours for help and ignored the "join our club" pleas. I was a commuter. I traveled from home to school to work, back and forth every day of the week. I never had time for anything.

I was so nervous to go to office hours but after chatting with a professor for a bit I realized they were just as human as I was and they genuinely showed interest in me and my story. After that, I opened up to visiting my professors for help. With their guidance, I was able to graduate in three and a half years.

I also had a great support team at work where I was working as a paraprofessional in my field. People are always willing to help one another if you let them.

Close

[![Maria Angelica Ruiz](../img/ruiz.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

* Develop habits that will make you a successful student
* Set healthy boundaries from family to make earning a degree a reality
* Seek and utilize financial aid, part-time work, and scholarships to pay for school
* Get involved in spaces on campus that will help keep them accountable for academic or personal goals
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I was incredibly fortunate to have received a phone call by an [EOP advisor](https://www.cpp.edu/~ssep/eop/index.shtml) and be invited to be a part of [Summer Bridge](https://www.cpp.edu/~ssep/summer-bridge/index.shtml). EOP was my academic gift that I always knew I wanted and did not know existed. I am grateful to the EOP staff that recognized my work ethic and potential. They taught and convinced me to look deeper within myself and completed the academic gaps my biological family could not help with. EOP also provided a much needed respite and home away from home. The achievement of earning my college degree, while affirming my desire to be a lifelong learner, led to the discovery and passion of helping others. EOP planted a seed of service, using the tools I learned that are applicable in both personal and professional areas of life. I now have the privilege to change lives as mine was transformed years ago by serving as the EOP Outreach & Admissions Counselor. EOP changed my family tree, literally, which is the root and core reason for its existence, such that we each leave a legacy for the next generation.

Close

[![Marilu Salcedo](../img/marilu-salcedo.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

My advice to you as an incoming first-generation college student is to never give up. There will be difficult times throughout your educational years, but always take a leap of faith by completing the job. If you have to work to meet financial needs take your time with your classes, and don't rush your success. Learning is a process and is best to gain knowledge rather than speed through the process. I know for myself, I wanted to finish as soon as possible because of the stress and personal problems I was going through during my bachelor's degree. Nevertheless, now that I look back, I made some changes for my master's. I took it slow and learned every detail from it. I continued to push through with the help of faculty and classmates. Furthermore, make sure you keep in contact with your classmates and meet to discuss class-related information, it is a tremendous help. In addition, get informed about the campus resources and try to enjoy college life as much as you can. CPP has a lot of activities and clubs to get involved in that you don't want to miss out on.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I am a proud Mexican American first-gen and first-language Spanish speaker. It took me an overall of 7 years as a transfer student to complete my bachelor's degree in Graphic Design. And 10 years after, I recently completed my Master's in Public Administration which took 4 years. The reason why it took long for each degree was because I had to work to provide for myself and my education. I did not have financial support from my parents for my education. But I pushed through despite the obstacles I was going through and managed to complete my bachelor's and master's degrees. In addition, I have been an example to those around me to go to school and complete their college degrees. They saw me as "if she can do it, I can do it too." It is never too late to go back to school and accomplish your dreams and goals. Success is on your hands when you are determined to accomplish a goal. Blessings on all your achievements and I know you will be fine, you got this!

Close

[![Dr. Cory Scott](../img/scott.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Don't be afraid! Whether it's asking questions, trying to get involved, going to a professor's office hours or asking for someone to be your mentor, don't be afraid. So many great opportunities are available but YOU must take the first step!
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

My college experience as a first-generation student was quite tough. Change is difficult. I was away from home for the first time, had so much pressure from my family and overall felt lost in this new world. I doubted myself and was afraid but could not show that to peers or family. Over time things improved substantially for a variety of reasons and opportunities on campus. I branched out and tried to seek help. In the process I learned how to embrace the changes I was faced with. There were times I felt like giving up but the constant support from instructors, advisors and peers on campus reassured me that I was close to the finish line. In addition, this achievement was not only for me but my family. The look my parents had when I walked across the stage still makes me emotional today. Receiving my degree was a symbol of their struggle, determination and hard work as much as it was mine.

Close

[![Nancy Mejia Terriquez](../img/terriquez.jpg)

section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

When I first graduated high school I was admitted to 3 CSUs. I decided to go to Sonoma State University. Before I even began classes, I was lost. I did not know that I needed to register for orientation, how to read my financial aid award, and how to select classes and which ones applied to my major. Everything was so new to me and, after my first semester, I decided to leave school. I ended up coming back to Southern California and began attending Mt. San Antonio Community College. There, I had the same experience and it took me 5 years to transfer out to Cal Poly Pomona. At CPP I was lucky enough to find people who cared about my success and guided me until the day I graduated.

Close

[![Dr. Jason Turcotte](../img/turcotte.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

Seek out your academic advisor within the first couple of weeks starting at Cal Poly Pomona. We will remember you and will have more time to address your needs, concerns, and fears than if you waited until registration. Even if you don't have a specific question yet, just stop in and say hello. Tell your advisor that you're the first in your family to go to college. It helps us understand your needs and how to help.
section: **What advice would you share with our first-generation college students?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I felt like a blank slate. I couldn't ask my parents, and they couldn't help me evaluate colleges and programs. I didn't know what questions to ask or things I should look for. When I arrived at campus, I initially felt that I had less life experiences than my classmates, so it took a little more time to make connections. Instead of focusing on the feelings of difference, I got involved. I became a campus tour guide and a writer for the school newspaper. Getting involved made me feel like a valued member of the community and that I could do anything that non first-generation students could do. I never communicated that I was a first-generation student but I utilized the time of my academic advisers and professors. Consistent visits to their offices is a big reason I was able to succeed.

Close

[![Dr. Greisy Winicki Landman](../img/winicki.jpg)
section: **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

title: I Am First Faculty & Staff Profiles
content:
## **What was your experience as a first-generation college student?**

I am the first college graduate of my family. The first year as an undergraduate was very scary: I did not know how to check for my classes, my grades, my progress, my financial aid. PLEASE contact your advisor on campus and also ask for help from the different offices that support you. Professors are great resources also, even if they are not your academic advisor. Connect with them. They want to know YOU!

Close

title: Our Partnerships
content:
## L.A. Region K-16 Collaborative

The L.A. Region K-16 Collaborative, convened by the nonprofit UNITE-LA, focuses on closing racial and gender gaps in postsecondary education and building pathways to careers in healthcare, engineering and computer science across Los Angeles County. Pomona Unified School District (PUSD), Citrus College, Mt. San Antonio College, and CPP work together to serve students in the East L.A. County subregion and increase access, retention and completion in the engineering and computer science fields.

[More Information](https://losangelesk16.org/%20 "opens a new window")

![Students pose at CPP Letters](img/partnerships/k-16.jpg)

![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-green-mobile.svg)
![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-green.svg)

title: Our Partnerships
content:
## Asian American, Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander Initiatives

AANHPI Initiatives creates opportunities for connection, cultural affirmation, and holistic success at Cal Poly Pomona. Supported by CSU ASAP funding, AANHPI Initiatives strives towards expanding student access, equity, and affordability and creating pathways to high-demand career opportunities for all students.
At CPP, AANHPI Initiatives collaborates with campus programs and organizations to celebrate culture, build community, and connect students to resources that promote academic achievement, personal growth, and overall wellbeing.

[More Information](https://www.calstate.edu/impact-of-the-csu/diversity/csu-asap%20)

![Asian student cheers at Commencement](img/partnerships/csu-asap.jpg)

title: Retention & Graduation Specialists
content: # Retention & Graduation Specialists

title: Retention & Graduation Specialists
content:
## About

Your Retention and Graduation Specialist (RGS) will help you stay on track toward graduation. If you have an Academic Warning, Academic Notice, or Academic Notice with Contract hold, you may be required to meet with your RGS to develop a plan to improve your GPA and continue to be enrolled at CPP.

Contact your RGS as soon as you see that you have a hold. This will help to you to get back on track faster, as well as help you to avoid long lines during registration periods.

Make an appointment with your RGS using [CPP Connect](http://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/cppconnect/index.shtml).

title: Student Communication
content: # Student Communication

title: Student Communication
content:
## About

The Office of Student Success is responsible for several dimensions of Cal Poly Pomona student communication.

title: Student Communication
content:
## Billy Chat

Billy Chat is the university's [artificial intelligence text-messaging robot](https://www.cpp.edu/~studentsuccess/billy-chat/).

title: Student Communication
content:
## Ask Billy

Ask Billy is the university's [question-and-answer database](https://cpp.service-now.com/askbilly).

title: Student Communication
content:
## Bronco Advising Center Social Media

The BAC posts explainers and reminders on [Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/cppbac/). It also shares information and events from other campus partners to amplify messaging.

title: Student Communication
content:
## How-to Videos

The BAC produces [how-to videos](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCRqnPcIL-oS24GmvMwZb0KA) that explain navigating the BroncoDirect Student Center.

title: Student Communication
content:
## BroncoBytes

The BAC writes and sends a [biweekly student newsletter](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/broncobytes/broncobytes.shtml) with announcements, important dates and deadlines, and frequently asked questions.

title: Student Communication
content:
## Targeted Communication

The Office of Student Success manages targeted phone and email campaigns that help students stay on a path to graduation.

title: Student Communication
content:
## Semester Conversion Student Communication

The Office of Student Success manages the student communication plan around [semester conversion](https://www.cpp.edu/~semester/).

title: Student Communication
content:
## Semester Conversion Student Communication

The Office of Student Success manages the student communication plan around [semester conversion](https://www.cpp.edu/~semester/).

# Project CAMINOS

![group of students walking by the CPP mural and a pair of prospective students with their parent](img/home-header.png)

title: Project CAMINOS
content:
## Welcome Future Broncos!

Project CAMINOS, which stands for **C**ultivating **A**ccess and **M**entoring through **I**nstitutional **N**etworks and **O**pportunities for **S**uccess, is dedicated to cultivating a college going culture amongst students interested in applying to Cal Poly Pomona. We help students, and their families, make attending college a reality.

title: About Us
content:
## Our Name

CAMINOS stands for Cultivating Access and Mentoring through Institutional Networks and Opportunities for Success.

title: About Us
content:
## What is Project CAMINOS?

Project CAMINOS was established in 2019 through a Title V Hispanic Serving Institute (HSI) grant through the Department of Education. The program is dedicated to eliminate the equity gap by preparing underrepresented students for success and graduation through three pillars:
1. Outreach
2. Advising and Transition Program such as the [Bronco Scholars Program](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/bronco-scholars/)
3. Faculty Development

Project CAMINOS seeks to support prospective students and their families through four workshop types.

title: About Us
content:
## Project CAMINOS Directory

| Dr. Cecilia Santiago-González | Associate Vice President, Student Success and PI, Project CAMINOS | csantiago@cpp.edu |
| Xiomara Melendez | Interim Director, College Engagement & Retention | xmmelendez@cpp.edu |

title: About Us
content:
## What is a Hispanic Serving Institute (HSI)?

The title of HSI is given to universities with a student body of at least 25 percent Hispanic and that is committed to ensuring their success. Cal Poly Pomona has approximately 27,000 students, and nearly half are Hispanic. Learn [more](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/access-and-transitions/index.shtml) about Cal Poly Pomona's HSI numbers.
section: What is a Hispanic Serving Institute (HSI)?

title: Recursos Para Padres y Familiares
content: # Recursos Para Padres y Familiares

title: Recursos Para Padres y Familiares
content:
## Sobre Nosotros

![Parents and family pose at College of Business Administration](../img/group-photo.jpg)

El programa Proyecto CAMINOS de Cal Poly Pomona está dedicado a ayudar a los futuros estudiantes y familiares con su selección y transición a la universidad. Sabemos que no es sólo una transición para su estudiante, pero también para usted.

Nunca es demasiado temprano para comenzar a hablar con su estudiante sobre la universidad. Ya sea que estén en la escuela primaria o secundaria, es importante mantenerse informado. Estamos aqui para apoyarlo con cualquier pregunta que pueda tener.

title: Recursos Para Padres y Familiares
content:
## Descubre Cal Poly Pomona

![A group poses together in the Student Services Breezeway](../img/parents2.jpg)

Aprenda cómo navegar la web de la universidad con este tutorial. Empiece su búsqueda con estos tres sitios:

Algunos de nuestros servicios incluyen:

* Proporcionar información sobre la universidad

Llamenos al (909) 869-4454 para acer una cita. Reciba nuestros anuncios al unirse a nuestra lista de contactos.

title: Parent and Family Resources
content: # Parent and Family Resources

title: Parent and Family Resources
content:
## About Us

![Parents and fanily pose on the steps of the CBA Building.](../parents-family.jpg)

Cal Poly Pomona's Project CAMINOS is dedicated to help prospective students and families with their college selection and transition. We know it is not only a transitition for your student, but for you as well.

It is never too early to start talking to your student about college. Whether they are in elementary or high school it is important to keep yourself informed. We are here to support you with any questions you may have.

title: Parent and Family Resources
content:
## Discover Cal Poly Pomona

![A group poses together in the Student Services Building breezeway](../img/parents2.jpg)

Learn how you can start to navigate college websites with this tutorial. Start your web search with these three websites:

[Outreach, Recruitment, and Educational Partnerships (OREP)](https://www.cpp.edu/outreach)

[Office of Admissions](https://www.cpp.edu/admissions)

[Financial Aid & Scholarships](https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/index.shtml)

title: Parent and Family Resources
content:
## Our Services

Some of our services include:

* College information
* How to apply to college
* How to apply for financial aid
* Major and career information

Schedule an appointment at (909) 869-4454. Receive email updates about our services:

[Subscribe](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/project-caminos/about-us/email-list.shtml)

title: Elementary Student Resources
content: # Elementary Student Resources

title: Elementary Student Resources
content:
## Welcome Teachers!

We hope your elementary school students enjoy learning about Cal Poly Pomona.

Cal Poly Pomona’s Project CAMINOS is a Department of Education funded program.

Our resources below give elementary school students an opportunity to learn about college.

Find resources such as campus exploration videos, Nearpod activities, coloring pages, and games!

title: Elementary Student Resources
content:
## Let's explore Cal Poly Pomona!

Our short videos are a great tool for elementary students to learn more about Cal Poly Pomona. Click on the video above to watch our introduction and access the playlist.

title: Elementary Student Resources
content:

section: Learn About CPP Through Our Game

title: CPP Lotería
content:
## How to Play

1. Select your playing tabla (card).
2. Claim your tabla (card) by renaming yourself on zoom with the playing tabla (card) number. Example: 03 Billy Bronco
3. Listen for the cards to be called.  Mark your virtual playing tabla (card) with a check mark on the left top corner once the card is called. Keep playing until you check mark ALL the cards in the playing tabla (card).
4. Be the FIRST to unmute to say out loud "**¡Lotería**!" or type in the chat "**¡Lotería**!".

title: Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year
content: # Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year
section: Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year

title: Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year
content:
## Want to graduate on time? Save money? Get better grades? Plan for 30 semester units every academic year to stay on track!

Most degrees at Cal Poly Pomona are 120 semester units. Taking 30 units per year puts students who are on these degree programs on track to graduate in two (for transfer students) or four years (for first-time freshmen). Engineering and architecture degrees have more units. [Work with your academic advisor](../directories/advising-directory.shtml) for longterm academic planning and determining your unit load.
section: Want to graduate on time? Save money? Get better grades? Plan for 30 semester units every academic year to stay on track!

title: Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year
content:
## Why Take 30 units per year?

* **Extra years of college can cost you thousands, both in what you pay and what you lose from not having a job**. Based on 2024-25 Cal Poly Pomona tuition costs, completing only 24 units per year in 5 years costs an extra $7,780. Completing only 20 units per year in 6 years costs an extra $15,560. (These figures exclude housing, parking and other school-related fees.)
* **According to reports from [Complete College America](https://completecollege.org/strategy/15-to-finish/), students who take 30 semester units per year tend to get higher GPAs.** When it comes to getting better grades, taking 30 units is a no-brainer.
* **Taking 30 semester units per year dramatically increases your chances of reaching graduation.** Plus, graduating on time means that you can take advantage of more opportunities outside of school, like being able to travel, volunteer or start a graduate degree.

title: Get in Gear! Take 30 Semester Units A Year
content:
## Ways to Take 30 Units A Year

There are several ways you can break up 30 units across an academic year. See below for some possible unit combinations.

* 15 units in fall + 15 units in spring
* 12 units in fall + 12 units in spring + 6 units in summer
* 12 units in fall + 15 units in spring + 3 units in summer
* 15 units in fall + 12 units in spring + 3 units in summer
section: Ways to Take 30 Units A Year

title: Student Success Fee
content:
# Student Success Fee

![What Is The Student Success Fee?](img/what_is.png)

title: Student Success Fee
content:
## Learn More About the SSF

The Student Success Fee (SSF) provides financial support to areas of the campus that have a significant impact on student retention and graduation. Additionally, it provides extra funding for new programs and improvements for information technology services to help students.

The fee goes towards initiatives such as updating equipment in classrooms and computer labs and expanding library hours on evenings and weekends. Click through the main categories below to learn how the Student Success Fee has been allocated to important projects and initiatives at CPP, and check out the [fee allocation page](fee.shtml).

See full spending and category details by reading through the annual SSF reports, which are available on the 'History' tab, or by clicking the button below.

[See Full Reports](history.shtml)

title: Academic Support
content:
# Academic Support

![A photo of a CBA lecture hall -- students listen to a female instructor in the center of the room](img/academic-support.jpg)

title: Academic Support
content:
## Academic Support

Students need high-tech classrooms, accessible library and tutoring services, and opportunities to get involved in their department. This support helps them achieve both personal and academic success. Read below for the ways that the Student Success Fee makes all of this possible.

![A white woman teaches a course. There is writing on the whiteboard behind her](img/improving-the-classroom.jpg)

title: Academic Support
content: ## Improving the Classroom Experience

title: Academic Support
content:
### Adding more high demand courses

Every term, we look at courses that fill up as students register, often leaving many students on waitlists. We used this data in 2021-22 to add 50 courses in the fall semester and 51 courses in the spring semester. This information is used in the future to offer classes students need to stay on track for graduation.

title: Academic Support
content:
### Replacing and updating equipment

Students need to learn how to use the tools they’ll need on the job after graduation. In the 2021-22 year, there were almost 25 projects across campus to modernize learning environments. Examples include new hyflex classrooms, expanding the success of the Bloomberg Financial Markets Lab, and raised bed sewing machines for the Footware Design and Merchandising program.

title: Academic Support
content:
### Bringing the classroom to life

In 2021-22, the fee funded 29 faculty projects where students embraced our learn-by-doing culture. Examples include collaborative audiobooks of Hispanic and Chicano authors in the United States, the College of Engineering podcast, and an on-campus wildlife learning experience.

![Two female students work on homework together in the Learning Resource Center](img/supporting-your-academic-success.jpg)

title: Academic Support
content: ## Supporting Your Academic Success

title: Academic Support
content:
### Expanding library hours and research desk services

The Library continues to adapt to students' learning needs. In 2021-22, the fee supported approximately 940 hours of student assistant time to provide expanded hours during the fall semester. [Visit the Library website](https://www.cpp.edu/library/index.shtml) to learn more about the online resources available to all students.
section: Expanding library hours and research desk services

title: Academic Support
content:
### Increasing free Learning Resource Center tutoring support

[The Learning Resource Center](https://www.cpp.edu/lrc/index.shtml) is the university’s flagship tutoring center. Tutors can help with homework questions and strengthening writing assignments. Students can even brush up on their time management skills or find ways to get motivated. In 2021-22, 66 LRC tutors connected with over 6,000 students across all colleges and majors. The LRC continued to offer Zoom and online tutoring.
section: Increasing free Learning Resource Center tutoring support

title: Academic Support
content:
### Evaluating student learning

CPP’s faculty and staff want to know what works and what needs improvement, whether it’s a homework assignment or an entire program. Faculty and staff assess student learning all year long. The fee has funded expenses related to staff and faculty development as well as ongoing assessment activities. In 2021-22, this also included an effort to assess General Education.

![Three students, two male and one female, cheer at a Commencement ceremony](img/enriching-your-path.jpg)

title: Academic Support
content: ## Enriching Your Path to Graduation

title: Academic Support
content:
### Implementing new first-year programs

We’ve ramped up programs that support students in the sciences, offer coaching and peer mentoring, and connect students to the campus community in a virtual format. These programs include the [STEM Success Program](https://www.cpp.edu/stemsuccess/index.shtml), [Maximizing Engineering Potential](https://www.cpp.edu/engineering/mep/index.shtml), [Bronco Scholars](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/early-start/bronco-scholars/index.shtml), [PolyTransfer](https://www.cpp.edu/polytransfer/index.shtml), [First Year Experience](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/fye/index.shtml), the [Student Innovation Idea Lab](https://www.cpp.edu/ilab/), [I AM FIRST](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/oss/i-am-first/index.shtml), [RAMP](https://www.cpp.edu/ramp/), and [Take 30](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/take30/index.shtml).

title: Academic Support
content:
### Expanding academic advising services

[Each college has a center](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/directories/advising-directory.shtml) where advisors help students plan long-term academic success and navigate university policies and processes. Over 2021-22, funding provided each College Student Success Center with workshops, drop-in hours, and assistance with advising tools and appointments. The [Bronco Advising Center](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/bronco-advising-center/index.shtml) advisors also support students with general academic advising services and the [Undeclared student community](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/bronco-advising-center/undeclared-advising.shtml). Additionally, funding was deployed for staff development to ensure that staff is updated on best practices related to student success and inclusivity.

title: Academic Support
content:
### Creating additional opportunities for student support/department involvement

Over 50 academic departments on campus continued to offer a wealth of activities for their students to participate in. This includes hiring student peer mentors, discipline-related conferences, industry speakers and seminars, and virtual graduation celebrations.
section: Creating additional opportunities for student support/department involvement

title: Academic Support
content:
50 academic departments on campus continued to offer a wealth of activities for their students to participate in. This includes hiring student peer mentors, discipline-related conferences, industry speakers and seminars, and virtual graduation celebrations.

title: Engagement Support
content:
# Engagement Support

![Students walk near Kellogg Arena during CPP Fest, a welcoming engagement event](img/cpp_fest.jpg)

title: Engagement Support
content:
## Engagement Support

Student involvement in clubs and organizations, support groups and other co-curricular programs are an important part of the college experience. Through departments like the Center for Transformation, Retention, Equity and Empowerment and the Student Project Lab, students have the opportunity to enrich their academic experience through community building and cultural awareness. Scroll down to learn how the Student Success Fee helps fund these programs and initiatives at CPP.

![](img/diademuertos.jpg)

title: Engagement Support
content:
## Supporting Student Retention

The Centers for Transformation, Retention, Equity and Empowerment (TREE) are stewards for the SSF. The centers support over 9,000 students each semester, and the SSF helps fund their operational needs, study supplies, facility preservation, student computer workstations, mentorship programs, staff-student coaching, and staff development.

On average, the SSF contributes to the success of 60 student programs in which more than 2,300 students are served.

---

**On the Diversity Ambassadors Program -**"DA has ultimately improved my world view; it has increased my self-awareness, and my love for my culture. I have learned more in Diversity Ambassadors this year than I have in my entire time spent in grade school history classes."

**On the Cultural Centers -** "At the Pride Center, I have a home and people who support me. I feel like the space benefits students in the community to stay on track to graduate."

![Various Athletic programs at CPP represented, including baseball, womens track and field, womens soccer and basketball](img/athleticsbanner.jpg)

title: Engagement Support
content:
## Supporting Bronco Pride

Intercollegiate Athletics supported approximately $1,200,000.00 in athletics grant-in-aid scholarships. With inflation being a factor in increased costs in personnel, travel, equipment and other services, Athletics held a higher reserve than normal to offset unknown increased costs.  The National Collegiate Athletic Association also granted additional years of eligibility to all student-athletes returning from COVID.  We held additional funds as we did not know exactly how this would affect squad sizes.

Intercollegiate Athletics changed the sport operational funding model for each team to reflect the California Collegiate Athletic Association’s (CCAA) championship participant allowances. Additional factors included budgeted facility improvement projects that did not occur due to higher than expected cost projections.

Additionally, the SSF supports annual payments from the recent construction of the Don Miller and Ron Simons Rose Float lab.

---

**Jayda Villareal, Women's Basketball -**"Being a part of a team at CPP has given me the support system that I was in desperate need of during my transition into a new chapter of my life as a college student athlete. I am so thankful I am able to consider my teammates as my family!"

**Ryan Knowles, Baseball -**"Being on a team at CPP has given me every experience an aspiring college student could want. I've formed life long friendships while also pursuing dreams, and making them a reality."

![Veteran students pose for the camera in front of military flags](img/veterans.jpg)

title: Engagement Support
content:
## Supporting Veterans

The Student Success Fee helps fund the [Veterans Resource Center](https://www.cpp.edu/veterans) (VRC), including services like computers and free-printing, peer support and guidance, scholarship opportunities, Veteran recognition events and career workshops geared towards the Veteran community.

In 2023, Cal Poly Pomona had over 1,500 military affiliated students enrolled at the university. Overall, it was the 4th largest enrollment of military affiliated students in the California State University system. The department was able to it expand its Horses for Heroes program by making it available to CPP’s student veterans for fall and spring terms.

The Center hired ten student employees that provided support and created community for CPP’s military affiliated students. In addition, the funds gave the VRC the opportunity to offer its very first Military Affiliated Graduation ceremony. It recognized and celebrated the academic success of Cal Poly Pomona’s students that are military family members.

---

**Kendall Haun, Public Administration (U.S. Air Force Dependent) -***"My father, a veteran, raised me and set a standard of what excellence looks like from a very young age. I'm blessed to have daily interactions with veterans at the Veterans Resource Center where the excellence remains."*

**Anthony Betancourt, Electronics System Engineering (U.S. Air Force) -** *"Having the opportunity to meet veterans has provided a greater appreciation of the sacrifices they've made as well as a greater understanding of the history  of the United States."*

content:

title: History of the Student Success Fee
content:
# History of the Student Success Fee

The Student Success Fee was recommended to the CSU Chancellor’s Office for approval at the conclusion of an extensive consultation process with students from Oct. 16 through Nov. 2, 2012. In total, 40 presentations were made to a wide array of students groups, including four open forums that all students were invited to attend. 1,385 students participated in the consultation process by attending a student-led presentation and completing a questionnaire that included whether they were in support of the new fee.

Results of the submitted questionnaires indicated that **71.2 percent of students who participated were in support of the Student Success Fee**. The student-led Fee Advisory Committee voted unanimously to recommend the fee. From there the California State University Chancellor’s Office gave final approval to the proposed Student Success Fee for Cal Poly Pomona, which went into effect Fall Quarter 2013.

**Fee Implementation Schedule**

*Note: The Student Success Fee is not assessed in Summer if summer courses are administered through the College of the Extended University.*

* Beginning Summer Quarter 2013, the SSF Student Success Fee was implemented at a rate of $74.00/quarter
  + Summer Quarter 2014 adjusted to $102.00/quarter, plus annual inflationary rate adjustment by using the Higher Education Price Index
  + Summer Quarter 2015 adjusted to $129.00/quarter, plus the annual inflationary rate adjustment by using the Higher Education Price Index
  + Summer Quarter 2016 adjusted to $138.25/quarter, plus the annual inflationary rate adjustment by using the Higher Education Price Index
* Beginning Summer Quarter 2017 and subsequent years, the Student Success Fee is adjusted based on the annual inflationary rate adjustment by using the Higher Education Price Index.

**Annual Reports (By Fiscal Year)**

### Committee Oversight

The University President will annually form and charge the Student Success Fee Oversight Executive Committee. The Executive Committee will be made up of the Provost/Vice President for Academic Affairs, Vice President for Student Affairs, Chief Information Officer (or their respective designees), as well as three students appointed by Associated Students, Inc, and three members of the faculty, appointed by the Academic Senate.

Technology is a crucial piece of the educational landscape today. As we return to more in-person instruction on campus, we know that WiFi, computer labs, and tech support are more important than ever. We’ve used the Student Success Fee to bolster these efforts in the following ways.

title: Community Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Have Community Wellness?

Community wellness focuses on the wellbeing, health and education of a specific group or society. Community wellness seeks to address a combination of economic, environmental, cultural and political conditions of the individual that would help them flourish and fulfill their potential.

Through community wellness, individuals can improve their connectedness to the community, advocate for positive changes that would benefit their society and create programs to encourage resiliency.

**Tips to Maintain Community Wellness**

* Volunteer for charities and local organizations
* Sign up for community clean up events
* Join community groups centered around your hobbies
* Organize outings and gatherings
* Engage is self-care regularly
* Attend counseling if needed
* Engage in group exercise classes
* Visit community centers
* Attend peer support groups

# Emotional Wellness

* [CPP](https://www.cpp.edu)
* [JED Campus](index.shtml)

* [...](index.shtml)

title: Emotional Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Be Emotionally Well?

Emotional wellness means feeling most of the time that we can pursue fulfilling experiences, respond to challenges, connect with others, and experience internal acceptance of ourselves. We are wired to feel a range of emotions, such as sadness, anger, elation, and uncertainty.

Emotional wellness means being able to acknowledge all these feelings, as they seek to inform us about what we might need to look after ourselves. When we are emotionally well, we feel empowered to acknowledge the full range of our emotions, take care of our emotional needs in the moment, and continue relating to others and pursuing our goals.

**Tips to Maintain Your Emotional Wellness**

* Ensure you sleep 7-8 hours every night. Adequate sleep lets us cope better with stress, difficult emotions, and solving problems. Too much sleep can make us groggy and worsen problems if we avoid them.

* Eat 3 meals throughout the day and maintain a balanced diet. Stay hydrated. Our physiology and emotions are closely intertwined: If you are shaky because of hunger or low blood sugar, our brain can interpret that as anxiety or powerlessness.

* Set aside time to “be with your emotions.” You can try journaling, taking a reflective walk, listening to music matching your feelings, talking to a friend, making art (it does not have to be good), or hitting the gym.

* Be honest with yourself about any emotions that might be present – We do not get to choose our emotions, only how we respond to them. By "bottling up” painful feelings we miss the opportunity to learn what we need to care for ourselves in the moment.

* Connect with others and talk about your feelings. Yes, opening up can feel vulnerable and scary. It also creates opportunities for feeling understood, supported, less isolated, and builds emotional intimacy. Sometimes we do not need to talk at all, and it just feels good to be around someone. Being independent and maintaining emotional wellness does not mean doing everything on your own. It means having the ability to recognize when you need to care of yourself by seeking support from others.

* Make time for fulfilling activities outside of school. If school is all you have going on, and it becomes stressful, 100% of your life is stressful. But if you have other activities in your life, they can balance the stress of school and give your brain a much-needed break to consolidate information.

* Ensuring emotional wellness consists of a lot of preventative maintenance. You do not have to wait for “rock bottom” to reach out for guidance. Consider seeking input from someone you trust, mentor, or mental health provider if you feel it might be helpful to get additional input in sorting through your emotions.

**Need help now? Call or text 988 — the National Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. You can also chat online at [suicidepreventionlifeline.org/chat/](https://chat.988lifeline.org/). You'll be connected with a trained counselor and are available 24/7 for people in crisis and those who support people in crisis.**
section: What Does it Mean to Be Emotionally Well?

# Financial Wellness

* [CPP](https://www.cpp.edu)
* [JED Campus](index.shtml)
* Financial Wellness
* [...](index.shtml)

title: Financial Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Be Financially Well?

Overall, being financially well in college typically means you have a good grasp of budgeting and managing finances, such as paying bills on time, avoiding unnecessary spending, and possibly investing some money, if able. You might have a steady source of income, such as a part-time job, or freelance work.

It could also mean planning ahead, including having a rainy-day fund or savings for future goals like study abroad or a post-graduation move.

**Tips to Maintain Your Financial Wellness**

##### **Create and Stick to a Budget**

* Tracking income and expenses is an essential skill. Start by understanding how much money you have coming in (from jobs, financial aid, or elsewhere) and what your expenses are (like rent, food, and textbooks).
* Allocate a specific amount for each category (e.g., groceries, entertainment) and stick to it. There are budgeting apps like Mint or YNAB that can help manage your finances.

##### **Avoid Unnecessary Debt**

* If you have a credit card, use it responsibly. Pay off the balance each month to avoid interest charges, and never spend more than you can afford to pay back.

**Build an Emergency Fund**

* Start small! Aim to save a few hundred dollars as a starting point. This fund can cover unexpected expenses like medical bills or car repairs.
* You can also set up automatic transfers from your checking account to a savings account to build your emergency fund gradually.

**Find Part-Time Work or Freelance Opportunities**

* Look for a part-time job that fits with your academic schedule. On-campus jobs can be particularly convenient. Find one by visiting the Career Center or visiting [www.cpp.edu/career](http://www.cpp.edu/career).
* Explore freelancing, if able. If you have skills like writing, graphic design, or coding, consider freelancing. Platforms like Upwork or Fiverr can help you find gigs.

**Take Advantage of Student Discounts**

* Use your Bronco One Card to get discounts on everything from software and subscriptions (like Spotify) to clothing and travel.

**Live Within Your Means**

* Cook at home or in your residence to save money. Meal prepping can make this easier and more efficient.
* It’s easy to overspend on social activities. Look for free or low-cost events on or around campus, and consider hosting get-together. with friends and family at home rather than going out.

##### **Plan for the Future**

* Start investing early. If possible, open a Roth IRA or start contributing to a savings account that can grow over time. Even small amounts add up.

* Think about how your financial decisions now will affect your future, whether it’s avoiding excessive debt or saving for post-graduation plans.
section: What Does it Mean to Be Financially Well?

title: Physical Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Be Physically Well?

Physical wellness consists of maintaining regular movement, consuming balanced nutrition, getting adequate sleep, and maintaining proper hydration. It also emphasizes the importance of preventing illness and effectively managing chronic conditions.

By actively caring for your body today, you help ensure its health and vitality for the future, by laying a strong foundation for overall wellbeing.

**Tips to Maintain Your Physical Wellness**

* Maintain a regular sleep schedule and get between 7-9 hours of sleep each night.

* Practice 30 minutes of movement at least 5 days a week.

* Focus on a variety of foods including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats. Limit processed foods, sugary drinks, and excessive salt.

* Schedule regular health check-ups and screenings to catch potential issues early and stay informed about your health status.

* Drink plenty of water throughout the day.
section: What Does it Mean to Be Physically Well?

title: Social Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Be Socially Well?

Social wellness refers to the quality and strength of relationships and interactions we have with others. It involves building and maintaining positive, supportive connections, having meaningful interactions and being able to communicate effectively. Social wellness also includes the ability to manage and navigate social environments while contributing to the wellbeing of the community.

For college students, maintaining social wellness is essential due to the pressures of academic life, new environments and growing independence.

**Tips to Maintain Your Social Wellness**

**Build a Support System**

* Surround yourself with positive relationships — friends, family or mentors who support and uplift you.
* Join clubs, organizations or groups with shared interests to foster meaningful connections.

**Develop Conflict Resolution Skills**

* Understand that conflicts are part of life, and learning how to resolve disagreements constructively is key to maintaining healthy relationships.
* Focus on effective communication and finding compromises when disputes arise.

**Engage in Community Activities**

* Volunteering or participating in campus events can help you meet new people and feel connected to the broader community.
* Involvement in community work fosters a sense of belonging and purpose.

**Practice Active Listening and Communication**

* Be open to listening and understanding others' perspectives without judgment.
* Improve your communication skills by being clear, assertive, and empathetic.
section: What Does it Mean to Be Socially Well?

title: Spiritual Wellness
content:
## What Does it Mean to Be Spiritually Well?

Spiritual wellness consists of finding a sense of purpose, connection, and inner peace in one’s life. For college students, this often means exploring personal beliefs, values, and a sense of meaning beyond academic or social pressures. It involves nurturing a connection to something greater than oneself, whether through religion, personal philosophy, or a commitment to a cause.

Cultivating spiritual wellness can help students build resilience, manage stress, and develop a deeper understanding of their place in the world. It encourages self-reflection and growth, contributing to overall well-being and a balanced life.

**Tips to Maintain Your Spiritual Wellness**

* **Explore Your Beliefs:** Take time to reflect on your personal values, beliefs, and what gives your life meaning. This might involve reading, meditating, or engaging in discussions with others who share similar or differing perspectives.
* **Practice Mindfulness:** Engage in mindfulness or meditation practices to connect with your inner self and cultivate a sense of peace. Regular mindfulness can help you stay grounded amidst academic pressures and personal challenges.
* **Seek Community:** Join campus groups or organizations that align with your spiritual interests or values. Being part of a community can provide support, a sense of belonging, and opportunities for growth.
* **Balance Your Life:** Make time for activities that nourish your spirit, such as volunteering, spending time in nature, or pursuing creative outlets. Finding balance between academic responsibilities and personal fulfillment is key.
* **Set Aside Reflection Time:** Dedicate time regularly for self-reflection or journaling. Reflecting on your experiences, goals, and feelings can help you stay connected to your spiritual self and navigate challenges with greater clarity.
section: What Does it Mean to Be Spiritually Well?

### Why has registration been rescheduled?

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### How do I know which courses can count for GE?

The BroncoDirect Class Search has Course Attribute filters, which you can use to look for specific GE designations. You can also review a course’s details to see if it has a specific GE designation. You can also see the General Education list in the [University Catalog](https://catalog.cpp.edu/preview_program.php?catoid=65&poid=17161). You can [review your Degree Progress Report in your Student Center](https://nam11.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.youtube.com%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DzXpEbJ3rApg&data=05%7C02%7Civalencia%40cpp.edu%7C9fe80f35c73d474270a508dc3980b471%7C164ba61e39ec4f5d89ffaa1f00a521b4%7C0%7C0%7C638448472156354954%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=qBpzae4NmCK1r3LD8yXSqJiCCBK5OH9Wkiyh%2BvtJ2dM%3D&reserved=0) to see which GE areas you may need to work on.

 If you have additional questions about General Education, contact the [Bronco Advising Center](http://www.cpp.edu/bac).
section: How do I know which courses can count for GE?

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### How much will summer tuition and fees cost?

The charts will be available on the [Student Accounting & Cashiering Services website](https://www.cpp.edu/student-accounting/index.shtml "https://www.cpp.edu/student-accounting/index.shtml") in the coming weeks.
section: How much will summer tuition and fees cost?

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### Will I be able to enroll in both major and GE courses?

Yes. You will be able to review the tuition and fee charts to estimate your costs. If you’re enrolling in non-GE courses, your per-unit cost will be different.
section: Will I be able to enroll in both major and GE courses?

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### How will I know if I’m eligible for summer financial aid?

Enroll in 6+ units, and [Financial Aid & Scholarships](https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/other-programs/summer/index.shtml "https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/other-programs/summer/index.shtml") will assess your eligibility. Monitor your email and your financial aid page in your Student Center for updates.
section: How will I know if I’m eligible for summer financial aid?

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### Will Summer classes be virtual?

All classes being offered in the three summer sessions will be offered in a mixture of online and in-person format.

content:

For specific questions about Summer Session, email summer@cpp.edu.
section: I have general questions about Cal Poly Pomona. Who can I contact?

content:

Visit the **[housing website](https://www.cpp.edu/housing/futureresidents/apply-to-uhs.shtml)** for more information.

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
### What is the Library summer schedule?

The University Library is open for the summer semester.  Check the [library homepage](https://www.cpp.edu/library/index.shtml) for current hours and to search for books or journals. The computer lab at the east entrance is open 24/7; Bronco ID is required for entrance to the lab.
section: What is the Library summer schedule?

content:

Yes, summer hours for dining locations around Cal Poly Pomona campus can be found **[here](dining.shtml)**.

title: Summer Session FAQs
content:
