title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
# Silver Streak at CPP

![Silver Streak is at CPP](img/2023_silverstreak.png)

![](img/silver_streak_cell_phone.png)

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
## An Easier Way to Get Around

Get to top destinations, including Downtown LA, faster than ever with the Foothill Transit Silver Streak bus, now boarding on campus at the Student Services Building!

Silver Streak buses stop on campus **between 7 a.m. and 9 p.m. on weekdays** at the stop located at the Student Services Building (Bldg. 121) drop-off circle.

You can track the Silver Streak in real time using the **myStop Mobile app**, available on both the App Store and Google Play Store. Just select 'Foothill Transit' as the agency and select Silver Streak. The app also has service alerts on detours or delays.

Learn more about Silver Streak on the [Foothill Transit website](http://foothilltransit.org/silverstreak/).

[See Silver Streak Map & Schedule](silver-streak-map-and-schedule.pdf)
section: An Easier Way to Get Around

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
## Class Pass Works on Silver Streak

Yes, the CPP Class Pass works on Silver Streak bus lines. If you already have your free Class Pass, there's nothing you need to do. Just board a Silver Streak bus and tap to board!

If you still need to pick up your Class Pass at the **BRIC front desk (Bldg. 42).,** click the link below for more info.

[Get a Class Pass](classpass.shtml)

![Tap Card CPP](img/cpp-tap-card---final1.png)

![](img/silverstreakmap.png)
section: Class Pass Works on Silver Streak

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
## Silver Streak Stop Location SSB Drop Off Circle

Look for the Silver Streak bus stop sign located at the drop off circle right outside of the Student Services Building (SSB #121). Silver Streak buses will stop on campus **between 7 a.m. and 9:30 p.m. on weekdays** at this stop.

You can track the Silver Streak in real time using the **myStop Mobile app**, available on both the App Store and Google Play Store. Just select 'Foothill Transit' as the agency and select Silver Streak. The app also has service alerts on detours or delays.
section: Silver Streak Stop Location SSB Drop Off Circle

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
## Frequently Asked Questions

Expand/Collapse All Frequently Asked Questions

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### When does the bus stop on campus?

Silver Streak buses will stop at Cal Poly Pomona on weekdays between 7am and 7pm, except on holidays. Visit www.foothilltransit.org for complete schedules.
section: When does the bus stop on campus?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### How long does it take to get to DTLA?

Trips on the Silver Streak to downtown Los Angeles can take between 45 minutes to an hour depending on things such as traffic, boardings, and detours.
section: How long does it take to get to DTLA?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### Does the Class Pass work on Silver Streak?

Yes, the Silver Streak is included in the Class Pass. If you're a Cal Poly Pomona student and you don't have a pass yet, you can pick one up at the **BRIC (Bronco Recreational Intramural Complex), Bldg 42.** -- just bring your ID and they'll take care of the rest.
section: Does the Class Pass work on Silver Streak?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### Where can I go on the Silver Streak?

The Silver Streak travels along the 10 freeway between Montclair and downtown Los Angeles. Some of the places along the route include the Montclair Transit Center, Pomona Transit Center, Plaza West Covina, El Monte Station, Union Station, Olvera Street, Walt Disney Concert Hall, Museum of Contemporary Art, Crypto.com Arena, and LA Live.
section: Where can I go on the Silver Streak?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### Do you have real-time info to see when the next bus will be there?

You can get real-time information by visiting [www.foothilltransit.rideralerts.com](http://www.foothilltransit.rideralerts.com) or by downloading the myStop Mobile app on Android or Apple devices. The app lets customers plan a trip, locate our buses on a map, and find the ETA of the bus nearest you.

To use it, select our agency in the list of agencies in the app (if you’re using it via the website you can skip this step) and select the bus line(s) or bus stop(s) you’re interested in. Then, it’ll show you the ETA of the next bus. The app also has service alerts on any detours or delays that may be impacting service.

 You can also track your buses using other apps like Transit or Moovit.
section: Do you have real-time info to see when the next bus will be there?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
### Why did you add this stop?

The changes we made to the Silver Streak are part of a pilot. This means that we’re trying this new routing and we’ll consider whether or not to make the changes permanent after the trial period.

One of the reasons that we are testing out this new bus stop is to give more improve connectivity and provide more options for students at Cal Poly Pomona. Making education more accessible for students is an important part of the service we provide. It also helps reduce congestion for people who live and work around campus, so we see college service as a win-win.

Let Foothill Transit know what you think of the new stop. You can send your feedback via email by writing to comment@foothilltransit.org or on their social channels (@foothilltransit).
section: Why did you add this stop?

title: Silver Streak at CPP
content:
the new stop. You can send your feedback via email by writing to comment@foothilltransit.org or on their social channels (@foothilltransit).

## About Us

The Centers for Transformation, Retention, Equity and Empowerment (T.R.E.E.) warmly welcome any person, regardless of how they identify. These spaces are designed to inspire exploration, learning, connection, and collaboration, with a core dedication to fostering student success.

Grounded in a profound appreciation for diversity in all its forms, the Centers for T.R.E.E. remain unwavering in their commitment to cultivating a respectful, inclusive, and empowering environment where every individual has the resources and support needed to flourish and thrive.

title: Who We Are
content:
## About the Centers for T.R.E.E.

The primary goal of cultural and identity support services through T.R.E.E. is to provide all students with resource assistance, advocacy, and services that provide exploration and celebration of all identities by building a sense of belonging.

**Key Offerings**

* Navigation assistance for campus resources
* Student wellbeing and mental health services
* Reflection and meditation room
* Career readiness and internship support
* Community outreach and engagement initiatives
* Educational workshops, programs and discussions on inclusive excellence
* Funding support for community-building celebrations and programs

**Retention Centers**

Retention centers through T.R.E.E. offer a variety of support aimed at supporting students' academic success, personal development, sense of belonging and overall well-being. These services are designed to address equity gaps related to student retention and graduation.

**Services Include:**

* Academic coaching and guidance
* Academic support services referrals
* Peer-to-peer tutoring and mentorship programs
* Student success workshops and event series
* Leadership development opportunities
* Student-centered celebrations and programs
* Community-building events and engagement
* Support and resources for student clubs and organizations
* Connections to peer support networks
* Networking opportunities and professional connections

# Asian & Pacific Islander Student Center

![An APISC tabling event](img/apisc-sideshow-pic.jpeg)

title: Asian & Pacific Islander Student Center
content:
## Mission Statement

The Asian & Pacific Islander Student Center promotes social justice, equity, and student success through education and community engagement, affirming and celebrating cultural identities within the Asian, Pacific Islander, and Desi diaspora.

The APISC warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: Center Mural
content:
## About the Mural

![The mural](../img/center-mural.png)

In the fall of 1997, Professor Haiming Liu collaborated with Lui Amador, coordinator of the Asian & Pacific Islander Student Center, and Eliseo Silvia, a Southern California Filipino American muralist, to offer a course entitled Asian American Murals.  In this class, eighteen students who were Asian, Pacific Islander, and non-API in ethnic descent, learned about different Asian American murals while researching contemporary issues facing the API community today.  Students participated in workshop-style sessions that helped them determine the best material to use for a mural.  The students then determined the themes and issues in API cultural and political history that felt most relevant to them.  By working with closely with Eliseo Silvia, these students were instrumental in determining the final image of the mural, and even painted the mural itself in April 1998. Currently, the 20-foot mural stands at the entrance of the APISC, welcoming the Cal Poly community into our space.

The goal of our project is to educate the community on a few of the prominent Asian American and Pacific Islander murals within the greater Los Angeles County. It is our hope that through understanding these murals, people will gain a better sense of the history of activism Asians and Pacific Islanders come from, as well as the struggles and achievements that the API community has experienced. After countless hours of exploring the many different API communities through art and conversing with artists and those knowledgeable about these murals, we are proud to present to you a glimpse of the heart, mind and soul that build and maintain our diverse communities.

title: Center Mural
content:
proud to present to you a glimpse of the heart, mind and soul that build and maintain our diverse communities.

content:

title: Resources
content: ## Discover our Resources

title: Resources
content:
### Community Space

Need a place to relax, study, or print between classes? Our physical space provides our community with access to a range of resources, including:

* Gender inclusive restroom with free tampons, liners, pads, and condoms
* Community space for hanging out and studying
* Computer lab with access to printers
* Community kitchen, including a fridge, microwave, and utensils
* Conference room to study, practice presentations, or meet as an organization

These resources are open to all students to utilize while our center is open.

title: Resources
content:
### Leadership and Volunteer Opportunities

Interested in getting more involved on campus and in the API community? Whether it’s mentoring other Broncos, volunteering at an event, participating on one of our event committees, or planning your own events through your club, there are always ways to get involved and stay connected to our center.

Come by the APISC and chat with our student leaders and coordinator to learn about more ways to get involved!

title: Resources
content:
### Resource Library

The APISC Resource Library is free and open to the campus community. Asian and Pacific Islander-related books and videos are available for classroom instruction, research, personal enrichment and education. Books are loaned out for one month and videos are due back after one week. Any student assistant or the coordinator of the center will be more than happy to assist you in locating a specific book or video. If we do not carry a specific book or video, please let us know and we will put it on our list of books to order for the future. Please see below for a list of our books, journals and other reference materials. You can come to the APISC to browse our selection of audio and video resources.

title: Black Resource Center
content:
# Black Resource Center

![Students pose for the camera](img/brc2.jpeg)

title: Black Resource Center
content:
## Mission Statement

The Black Resource Center (BRC) is committed to assisting African/African American/Black college students in successfully completing their degree programs. The center provides retention and outreach programs by providing academic, personal, and professional services and culturally-relevant programming.

**Vision**

The BRC seeks to strengthen the understanding of the Black Diaspora.

**Values**

* Empowerment
* Respect
* Accountability

The BRC warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: History of the Center
content: # History of the Center

title: History of the Center
content:
## Center History

![Students in the BRC](../img/brc-collage-banners-01.png)

The African American Student Center opened around 1995 according to the State of California Senate Certificate of Recognition letter presented to the center on May 7, 2015, which honored its 20th anniversary of operation.

On April 8, 2023, the center's name officially changed to the Black Resource Center (BRC).

[Name Change Timeline](../img/cultural-identity-center-name-change-timeline.docx)

title: Celebrating Black Student Achievement
content: # Celebrating Black Student Achievement

title: Celebrating Black Student Achievement
content: ## Celebrating Achievement

title: Celebrating Black Student Achievement
content:
### CBSA

The **Celebrating Black Student Achievement**initiative was created with the purpose of celebrating a student's academic achievement and improvement in any given academic term.

The CBSA initiative celebrates any student regardless of their race, color, national origin or sex who have earned at least a 3.0 GPA in the most recent academic term, OR who have improved their GPA by at least 0.5 points within the last two most recent academic terms.

content:

title: Black History Month
content: # Black History Month

title: Black History Month
content:
## About BHM

Black History Month is an annual celebration of the achievements of Black people throughout history. The BRC hosts a variety of programs that focused on culturally engaging spaces for Black students, elevating Black identities/lived experiences, and showcasing the current achievements of Black students.

title: Diversity Ambassador Program
content: # Diversity Ambassador Program

title: Diversity Ambassador Program
content:
## About the Program

![Students in Washington DC](../img/diversity-ambassadors-program.png)

Founded in 1999, the **Diversity Ambassador Program** is an academic program that supports Cal Poly Pomona’s mission to advance knowledge by linking theory and practice to prepare students for lifelong learning and careers in a changing multicultural world. The program was formed to educate CPP students about the significance of the Civil Rights Movement.

Diversity Ambassadors are students who travel on an alternative educational spring break. Our goal is to have students of any racial, ethnic and cultural background travel to the east coast of the United States of America to visit historical monuments, museums, Black colleges, churches, and institutions related to the emergence of the Civil Rights Movement.

The program strives to cultivate student leaders who are focused on social injustice and bettering their community through their involvement.

title: Kwanzaa Celebration
content: # Kwanzaa Celebration

title: Kwanzaa Celebration
content:
## Celebrating Kwanzaa at CPP

![Celebrating Kwanzaa](../img/kwanzaa-celebration.jpg)

Created in 1966 by Ron Maulana Karenga, professor and chair of African Studies at CSU Long Beach in response to the 1965 Watts Rebellion and as an alternative to commercialism. Gift giving is primarily for children. Educational items, books, and heritage symbols are often given.

Kwanzaa comes from 'kwanza,' a Swahili word meaning 'first,' and is used in the phrase 'mutunda ya kwanza,' which means 'first fruits or harvest.'

In addition to the services below, the BRC offers:

* And more...

title: Services
content:
### Study and Meeting Space

The BRC lounge and study room can be reserved for registered club and organization meetings and study groups. Students are always welcome to come by to relax in the center and take advantage of quiet study time in one of the study rooms.

title: Services
content:
### Library

A library of Black/African American special-interest books, periodicals, videos, and DVDs are available to be checked out by students, staff, and faculty.

title: Services
content:
### Free Printing and Email Access

The BRC has ethernet connections for a fast connection to the Internet. Five PC's are also available for students to use for academic tasks.

title: Services
content:
### Tours

Everyone is welcome to enjoy the benefits of the BRC. Come by any day during open hours for a tour and gain valuable information.

title: Services
content:
### Mentorship with Claremont Links

Learn more about the Claremont Area chapter of the Links, Inc.:

<https://claremontarealinks.com/home>

title: Services
content:
### Leadership Development

The Black Advance Leadership Caucus

Members of Black clubs and organizations receive valuable skills as they network with other students to form a support base for the academic year. These meetings are for current leaders, as well as students interested in learning more about themselves and their leadership potential. Students gain a background in leadership, discover how groups work and learn about program planning to enhance their personal and professional development.

The BRC works in collaboration with, provides resources to and/or advises the following organizations:

* Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc.
* Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc.
* African Student Association
* Association for the Advancement of Black Science Students (AABS)
* Black Business Student Association (BBSA)
* Black Student Union (BSU)
* Black Faculty & Staff Association (BFSA)
* Black Women of Excellence (BWE)
* Cal Poly Pomona Black Alumni & Friends Association
* Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc.
* Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Inc.
* National Pan-Hellenic Council (NPHC)
* National Society of Black Engineers (NSBE)
* Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc.
* Sigma Gamma Rho Sorority, Inc.
* Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc.

title: Services
content:
### Campus and Community Resources

Many students, staff and community members come to the BRC for resources on programming, events, health and wellness, and information on Black culture and African American history. BRC staff works to connect our community members by providing names, locations and contact information that will best serve them and provide them with the requested information.

BRC staff makes several tutoring referrals to the University Advising Center, Learning Resource Center and advisors of the academic colleges. Many collaborative programs and events are made possible by donations and involvement from the larger campus, Pomona and surrounding communities.

title: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education
content:
# César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education

![Students interact inside CECCHE](img/cecche2.jpg)
section: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education

title: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education
content:
## Mission Statement

CECCHE is committed to increasing the outreach, recruitment, retention, graduation and cultural pride of Chicanxs/Latinxs at Cal Poly Pomona.

CECCHE warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education
content:
### Nuestra Historia | Our Story

We exist because of a strong student activist movement aimed at establishing a more inclusive campus. The César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education (CECCHE) was the result of two years of demands by the Cal Poly Pomona RAZA students, supportive community representatives, and key Latinx leaders.

On May 7th, 1993, approximately 120 students gathered to form a campus-wide protest. Students marched through the campus carrying a coffin signifying the death of diversity on campus. Today, the coffin still stands in the CECCHE as a symbol of our past struggles, current victories, and the hope for a better tomorrow. The demands and protests resulted in the formation and establishment of the CECCHE in the spring of 1995.

title: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education
content:
### Areas of Focus

* Academic Success/ Graduate School Preparation
* Community Building
* Career Readiness
* Cultural & Arte
* Identity Development
* Social Awareness

title: César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education
content:
### Why We Embrace Latinx Terminology

Although we recognize that “Latino” has been able to center the experiences many communities, it also excludes non-binary people.

We use the alternative term “Latinx” to go beyond gender and establish inclusivity in our language and center.

title: Clubs and Organizations
content: # Clubs and Organizations

title: Clubs and Organizations
content:
## La Union Leadership Collective

La Union is a César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education leadership development program that serves as the representative and collaborative body for the Chicano/Latino student organizations, clubs, fraternities and sororities.

La Union is a space where Chicano/Latinx student organizations and clubs can learn from one another, take advantage of co-programming opportunities and can be informed of programs, services and events that each organization is planning through out the year.

Through La Union the César E. Chávez Center for Higher Education administrates the needs of the Cal Poly Pomona Chicano Latinx student leaders by providing leadership development through workshops, retreats and leadership and program advising.

### Phi Lambda Rho Sorority, Inc.

Phi Lambda Rho Sorority, Inc. is committed to developing and empowering strong Chicana/ Latina leaders who will make a positive impact in their community. As an organization, we strive to provide women with the resources to grow personally, academically, and professionally in order to reach their ultimate goal. ​ The purpose of Phi Lambda Rho Sorority, Inc. is to provide a lifelong sisterhood based on facilitating the advancement of women of color through higher education. ​ Our mission is to give back to our community, especially our marginalized youth while celebrating and promoting the Chicana/Latina culture.

The mission of the Latino Business Students' Association is to enable Latino students, as a whole, to increase their knowledge of the many opportunities in the business field and further develop each individual's skills and abilities through participation in club events. This includes our guest speaker series where get to hear from guest speakers experiences in the field of business, to workshops, and social mixers to expand our network. The main emphasis is placed on further developing business students as a whole. We want our members to be seen as Latino students who can professionally, personally and socially interact and participate successfully in the business world.

title: Clubs and Organizations
content:
### Hermanas Unidas de CPP

Hermanas Unidas is an organization that provides undergraduate, graduate, and professional leadership opportunities and resources to predominately Latina/Chicana students by participating in and organizing academic, community service, and social events/workshops with different chapters/clubs and orgs. Our organization strives to create a safe space for all of our members and encourage/welcome everyone with open arms.

title: Café con CECCHE
content:
# Café con CECCHE

![A Café con CECCHE event](../img/cecche1.png)

title: Café con CECCHE
content:
## Community and Coffee

Café con CECCHE aims to create a space where students can dialogue freely and openly about issues impacting them and their community. Topics that have been explored in the past include sex and relationships, Central American Caravans, machismo, self-care, mexicocentrism, and more. There are no limits to what is discussed, and your privacy will be respected.

content:

With more people observing the holiday, it is our goal to share the tradition and keep its integrity at the heart of the celebration. “Day of the Dead” has roots indigenous cultures and beliefs and is celebrated in the month of November. Celebrants make ofrendas, or offerings, to the spirits of loved ones who have died and place them on the community altar. This program is a...

title: Mindfulness Mondays
content: # Mindfulness Mondays

title: Mindfulness Mondays
content:
## Multiple - All Semester Long

*Mindfulness Monday’s* is a 30-minute biweekly mindfulness session held in collaboration with Cal Poly Pomona's Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS).

The workshops are designed to have participants start their week off calm, focused, and aware. Activities include, journaling, art, sound therapy, and meditation.

title: Pre-Med Peer Mentorship Program
content: # Pre-Med Peer Mentorship Program

title: Pre-Med Peer Mentorship Program
content:
## About

The *Pre-Med Peer Mentorship Program*was founded in order to further support students during their personal, academic, and professional medical journey. The program is designed for current Cal Poly Pomona students who identify as Latinx. It allows for connections between LMSA medical students and CPP students to provide support and guidance.

The Pre-Med Peer Mentorship Program is a year-long commitment for mentors and mentees alike. Through the mentorship program, mentors and mentees will commit to monthly scheduled meetings. The mentorship program will provide information and resources on career readiness, community-building skills, leadership development, networking skills, and more for mentees.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content: # Raza Graduation Celebration

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
## Raza Graduation Celebration

The Raza Graduation is voluntary and is open to all graduating students who would like to participate, regardless of race, sex, color, ethnicity or national origin.

**¡Felicidades! Congratulations, Class of 2025!**

**![Students at a CECCHE graduation celebration](../img/raza_grad.png)**

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content: ## Frequently Asked Questions

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### What is Raza Grad?

The annual Raza Graduation Celebration is a cultural event held towards the end of the academic year to celebrate the success of graduating Chicanx/Latinx students. This is a bilingual and bicultural celebration where family, friends, and community members come together to honor the graduating students.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### When and where will the ceremony be held?

The event will be held at the Kellogg Arena on the CPP campus on Sunday, May 4, 2025, at 1 PM
section: When and where will the ceremony be held?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### What is the best location to park, and is there a parking fee?

Parking structure 2 is a short 5-minute walk from the Kellogg Arena and will be the best place to park. It includes accessible parking and cart assistance will be given upon request. Parking is free.
section: What is the best location to park, and is there a parking fee?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will cart transportation be available for those who need it?

Yes, carts will be making rounds between parking structure 2 and the Kellogg Arena starting at 11 AM and will be available after the event has ended.
section: Will cart transportation be available for those who need it?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### How many guest tickets can each graduate request?

Due to limited seating, each graduate will receive 6 tickets. If more tickets become available, we will let graduates know.
section: How many guest tickets can each graduate request?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Can I reserve seats for guests?

Guest seating is on a first-come, first-served basis. There is no priority or reserved seating.
section: Can I reserve seats for guests?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### What if I, or a guest, require an accommodation?

When you register(ed) for the event on MyBAR, there was a question about accommodations. If you answered 'yes,' CECCHE staff will follow up with you before the event.

If you did not need accommodations at the time of registration but do now, please reach out via email at cecche@cpp.edu.
section: What if I, or a guest, require an accommodation?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will the event be livestreamed?

Unfortunately, the event will not be livestreamed. There will be a slideshow added to the CECCHE website to honor all the graduates that chose to participate.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Should I wear my cap and gown?

It is recommended that you wear all of your graduation regalia.
section: Should I wear my cap and gown?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will there be entertainment?

Yes, there will be performances before the ceremony begins.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will the event be translated?

The event will be in both English and Spanish.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will I be seated with my family?

Each graduate will have the opportunity to have one supporter join them on the floor. This supporter will be able to cross the stage with the graduate. All other guests will be sitting in the bleachers around the arena.
section: Will I be seated with my family?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Will I receive confirmation that I can participate?

You will receive a confirmation email within two weeks of submitting your registration form. You will also receive an email to confirm your participation about two weeks before the event.
section: Will I receive confirmation that I can participate?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### I registered but am no longer able to attend. What do I do?

If you are no longer able to attend, we ask that you email cecche@cpp.edu.
section: I registered but am no longer able to attend. What do I do?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Do I need to RSVP?

Yes, you needed to have registered via myBAR by March 28, 2025. The deadline has now passed.

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Is there a cost to participate?

There is no cost to participate in the ceremony.
section: Is there a cost to participate?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Who is eligible to participate in the celebration?

All CPP graduates from Fall 2024, Spring 2025, and Summer 2025 who have registered on MyBAR will be able to participate in the Raza Graduation Ceremony.
section: Who is eligible to participate in the celebration?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Can I attend both Raza Graduation and CPP Commencement?

Yes, participation in one does not conflict with the other. Please note, registering for Raza Graduation **does not** automatically register you to participate in the official CPP commencement ceremony, or vice versa.
section: Can I attend both Raza Graduation and CPP Commencement?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### Is the graduation ceremony only for spring graduates?

No, all graduates from Fall 2024 and Summer 2025 are able to register and participate in the ceremony.
section: Is the graduation ceremony only for spring graduates?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
### What time do graduates need to arrive for check in?

Graduates must arrive and check in outside of Building 17 by the steps by 11:30 AM. If you do not make it for the photo, you must check in at Kellogg Arena by 12:30 p.m. Please refer to the email that will be sent out two weeks before the event for final details.
section: What time do graduates need to arrive for check in?

title: Raza Graduation Celebration
content:
12:30 p.m. Please refer to the email that will be sent out two weeks before the event for final details.

title: Graduation Celebrations
content: # Graduation Celebrations

title: Graduation Celebrations
content:
## 2025 Celebrations

The Centers for T.R.E.E. Graduation Celebrations are campus-wide events that honor the diverse achievements and academic excellence of all students, celebrate their success and recognize their loved ones and the communities who supported them along the way.

All graduation celebrations are open and available to all, regardless of race, sex, color, ethnicity or national origin. Students may register for more than one celebration.

Join us in celebrating YOU! These ceremonies are not intended to replace Commencement, but offer an additional ceremony for those who wish to participate. We highly encourage participants to also apply for CPP’s official commencement graduation.

*\*Students who have graduated in Fall, are graduating this Spring or will be graduating in Summer are encouraged to register.*

**Ceremonies**

* Native Graduation Celebration
* Raza Graduation Celebration
* WRC Graduation Celebration
* Lavender Graduation Celebration
* Asian, Pacific Islander & Desi-American Graduation Celebration
* Black Graduation Celebration

title: Native American Student Center
content:
# Native American Student Center

![Students participate in a NASC event](img/nasc2.jpg)

title: Native American Student Center
content:
## Mission Statement

The mission of the Native American Student Center is to support the academic achievement and personal success of Native American students while promoting traditional culture at Cal Poly Pomona.

The NASC warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: Native American Student Center
content:
### Land Acknowledgements

We at Cal Poly Pomona respectfully acknowledge the original caretakers of this land, the Tongva peoples, and all of their ancestors, elders, and descendants, past, present and emerging. We also recognize this land known as Los Angeles County today is also home to many Indigenous peoples from all over, and we are grateful for the opportunity to live and work here as guests on these lands, the traditional, ancestral, and unceded territory of the Tongva.

title: Native American Student Center
content:
### Goals of the NASC

* Provide support to Native American students at Cal Poly Pomona.
* Outreach, recruitment, and retention and graduation of Native American students at Cal Poly Pomona
* Serve as resource to campus community on Native American issues.
* Create innovative learning-centered opportunities to develop students to become leaders in a global society.

title: Native American Student Center
content:
### Who is considered Native American?

American Indians, Alaska Natives and Native Hawaiians are members and descendants of sovereign Native Nations or the original Indigenous tribes of what is now known as the United States.

content:
## American Indian Science and Engineering Society (AISES)

The mission of the American Indian Science and Engineering Society (AISES) is to substantially increase the representation of American Indians and Alaskan Natives in engineering, science and other related technology disciplines. AISES works to promote, initiate and provide educational services for American Indian and Alaska Native pre-college, college and graduate students in STEM.

content:
## Learn More

![NASC Minor](img/minor.png)

A **Native American Studies minor** is offered through the Ethnic & Women’s Studies Department.

The program is especially critical given the history, presence, influence and role of Native Americans in the U.S. society and the ever increasing cultural and political connections among the Indigenous peoples of the world.

The minor provides an understanding of the histories, cultures and issues faced by Native Americans. It is ideal both for students who wish to learn about their own cultural heritages and for those who seek a greater understanding of Indigenous histories, cultures and communities in the U.S. and across the globe.

Students taking this minor will gain the interdisciplinary knowledge, tools and experiences to make a difference in our ever-changing, multicultural world.

For more information about the minor and its requirements, contact:

![](img/native-studies.jpg)

Chair

title: Community Building
content:
## Community Building Events

![Students attend a NASC event](../img/community.png)

To build community, we plan events that are intentionally focused on the three R’s that are integral to Native ways of being: Respect, Relations and Reciprocity.

These events include: Welcome Back Socials to kick-off each semester, Native Nights events including game nights, movie nights, or Mario Kart tournaments, Food and Finals Prep events to allow students to refuel and study together, and many others. All events are open to anyone in the campus community.

title: Professional Development
content: # Professional Development

title: Professional Development
content:
## Learn More

Our Professional Development Series allows us to collaborate with and highlight Native professionals in a variety of areas, particularly in STEM fields, to extend relations and opportunities to Native students. Past collaborations have included work with professionals from American Indian Science & Engineering Society, Southern California Edison’s Native American Alliance, Boeing, NASA, and Northrup Grumman.

These events provide our students with crucial connections to the workplace, individualized resume feedback, as well as internship opportunities and information on graduate school. All events are open to anyone in the campus community.

![Professional Development](../img/prodev.png)

title: Resources
content:
## Resources

Inclusion in this list does not constitute an endorsement by the NASC, nor does it assume responsibility for the information contained in those sites.

#### **[American Indian Graduate Center](https://www.nativeforward.org/)**

The American Indian Graduate Center (AIGC) is a national private 501(c)(3) nonprofit headquartered in Albuquerque, N.M., that provides fellowships to American Indian and Alaska Native graduate students throughout the United States.

#### **[American Indian Science & Engineering Society](http://www.aises.org/)**

Since 1977, AISES has worked to substantially increase American Indian and Alaska Native representation in science, technology, engineering and math (STEM) fields — as students, professionals, mentors and leaders. AISES employs a "full circle of support" model that begins with precollege programs, progresses into collegiate life, and continues into the professional years of members and into retirement.

#### **[Bureau of Indian Affairs](http://www.bia.gov/)**

Indian Affairs (IA) is the oldest bureau of the U.S. Department of the Interior. Established in 1824, IA currently provides services (directly or through contracts, grants or compacts) to about 1.9 million American Indians and Alaska Natives.

#### **[Indian Health Service](http://www.ihs.gov/)**

The Indian Health Service (IHS), an agency within the Department of Health and Human Services, is responsible for providing federal health services to American Indians and Alaska Natives.

#### **[National Congress of American Indians](http://www.ncai.org/)**

The National Congress of American Indians, founded in 1944, is the oldest, largest and most representative American Indian and Alaska Native organization serving the broad interests of tribal governments and communities.

#### **[National Indian Education Association](http://niea.org/)**

The National Indian Education Association advances comprehensive educational opportunities for American Indians, Alaska Natives and Native Hawaiians throughout the United States.

#### **[Office of Indian Education](http://www2.ed.gov/about/offices/list/oese/oie/index.html)**

The mission of the Office of Indian Education is to support the efforts of local educational agencies, Indian tribes and organizations, postsecondary institutions and other entities to meet the unique educational and culturally related academic needs of American Indians and Alaska Natives so that these students can achieve to the same challenging state standards as all students.

#### **[Red Book](http://www.cpp.edu/oslcc/native-american-student-center/docs/redbook_2014.pdf)**

The California Indian Culture and Sovereignty Center also developed the Red Book, a guide to assist Native American students, their families, and those who serve them to locate important educational resources.

**[Smithsonian National Museum of the American Indian](https://americanindian.si.edu/)**

A diverse and multifaceted cultural and educational enterprise, the National Museum of the American Indian (NMAI) is an active and visible component of the Smithsonian Institution, the world's largest museum complex.

**[Southern California Indian Center](http://www.indiancenter.org/)**

Established in 1969, the Southern California Indian Center, Inc. (SCIC) is a non-profit, community based organization dedicated to serving the American Indian, Alaskan Native, and Native Hawaiian communities of Los Angeles, Orange, and Riverside Counties. For the last 50 years, it has been our goal to promote self-sufficiency among our community. We provide much needed support and assistance in the areas of workforce development, youth education, family support, multimedia training so much more!

**[United American Indian Involvement](https://uaii.org/)**

United American Indian Involvement, Inc is the largest provider of human and health services for American Indians/ Alaskan Natives (AI/AN) living in the County of Los Angeles. Established in 1974 on the streets of skid row, downtown L.A. by two empowered American Indian women, Babba Cooper (Lakota) and Marian Zucco (Paiute) who wanted to serve the most impoverished community who lacked resources and access to services.

title: Services
content:
## Native American Library (print and media)

The NASC has developed a number of community partnerships. Among these is a partnership with the American Indian Resource Center in the Huntington Park Public Library (6518 Miles Avenue, Huntington Park, Calif.). The library has been generous in donating a wide range of printed materials about Native American culture to the NASC. These materials may supplement or augment materials in the Cal Poly Pomona Library and are available on loan to students working on research papers or projects involving topics related to Native Americans.

title: Pride Center

# Pride Center

![Pride Center inside](img/pride_inside.jpg)

title: Pride Center
content:
## Mission Statement

**The Pride Center** provides education, advocacy, support and a safe space for the lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, intersex, asexual, and the broader queer and gender expansive communities (LGBTQIA+) at Cal Poly Pomona.

Visit our space, connect with us, subscribe to our newsletter and attend our events to access the breadth of our offerings.

The Pride Center warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: Clubs and Organizations
content:
## Clubs and Organizations

#### **CPP Pride Club**

The Cal Poly Pomona Pride Club is a student-led organization dedicated to creating a safe, inclusive, and supportive environment for LGBTQ+ students and allies at California State Polytechnic University, Pomona

![Instagram Logo](https://www.cpp.edu/common/resources/img/social/instagram-60x60.png) [Follow Us On Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/cppprideclub)        [Register on myBAR](https://calpolypomona.campuslabs.com/engage/organization/cppprideclub)

####

#### **Black Queer Collective**

The Black Queer Collective at Cal Poly Pomona is dedicated to creating a welcoming and safe space where Black queer students can fully express and embrace their authentic selves.

![Instagram Logo](https://www.cpp.edu/common/resources/img/social/instagram-60x60.png) [Follow Us On Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/cpp_bqc)        [Register on myBAR](https://calpolypomona.campuslabs.com/engage/organization/blackqueercollective)

####

#### **oSTEM**

oSTEM is an organization for queer students and allies with the STEM fields (science, technology, engineering, math). They strive to educate, empower, and engage in a diverse community to identify, address, and advocate for the needs of LGBTQIA+ students in the STEM fields. They fulfill these needs through mentorship connections, networking opportunities, strategic collaborations, and professional leadership development. For more information, please contact them.

![Instagram Logo](https://www.cpp.edu/common/resources/img/social/instagram-60x60.png) [Follow Us On Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/cppostem/)      [Register on myBAR](https://calpolypomona.campuslabs.com/engage/organization/qsafe)

**QiB**

Queers in Business (QiB) is Cal Poly Pomona’s College of Business Administration's LGBTQIA+++ professional business association. Our goal is to cultivate a queer-positive business culture by bridging the gap between the queer business community and those in business outside the LGBTQIA+++ community. We do this by providing members with tools to foster inclusivity, diversity, and a strong sense of community within the business setting.

![Instagram Logo](https://www.cpp.edu/common/resources/img/social/instagram-60x60.png) [Follow Us On Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/qib_cpp/)

The definitions on this page are from, or informed by, this work.*

The definitions on this page are from, or informed by, this work.*

title: LGBTQ Terminology
content:
## **Background**

Keep in mind that as we explore and "define" these identities and terminologies, that many of them have come into existence due to the existence of a sex/gender/sexuality system that we have in place in our society. Many of these identities and terms are troubled/challenged by the existence of trans and nonbinary gender identities, intersex and transgender sexual expression/phenotypes and biological diversity.

**Sex/Gender/Sexuality System** (Rubin, 1984; Seiddman, 1995)

"Understandings of sexuality are predicated on beliefs about sex and gender. If there were no sex categories, current understandings of sexual orientation would not exist...Cultural beliefs about the sanctity of gender binarism naturalize a sex/gender/sexuality system in which heterosexuality is positioned as the only natural and desirable sexual form.” (Westbrook)

Furthermore, note that no identity has one singular, concrete defintion. Identities and language are fluid, and they continue to develop, shift, and evolve with the time. The knowledge presented here is simply our most educated and informed knowledge at the present time of writing.

title: LGBTQ Terminology
content:
## **Sexual Identity**

**Asexual** – A sexual orientation defined by a lack of sexual attraction. Asexuality is also a spectrum that varies between: allosexual (people who experience sexual attraction), asexual (no sexual attraction), gray asexual/grey-ace/[more microlabels exist] (rarely experience sexual attraction/have conditional sexual attraction), and demisexual (only experience sexual attraction after an emotional bond is formed).

**Multisexual Attraction Spectrum/M-Spec/Non-monosexual -**An umbrella term for identities that include sexual attraction to two or more sexes, genders, gender expressions, or combinations thereof. Non-monosexual. Some common identities under the M-spec umbrella are:

**Bisexual**

**Pansexual**

**Omnisexual**

**Polysexual**

**Monosexual**

**Gay/Homosexual/MLM/Achillean**

**Lesbian/Homosexual/WLW/Sapphic**

**Straight/Heterosexual**

title: LGBTQ Terminology
content:
## **Gender Expression**

**Androgynous**

**Femme/Feminine**

**Masc**

**Butch**

**Boi**

title: The Importance of Pronouns
content: # The Importance of Pronouns

title: The Importance of Pronouns
content:
## Understanding Pronouns

As part of our Equity, Diversity and Inclusion efforts at Cal Poly Pomona, we strive for inclusive excellence across the university. Respecting someone’s culture, identity, and/or life experiences, ***even if we don't fully understand***, is an easy way to create safety and establish a sense of belonging for all (students, faculty, staff etc.).

As a university, we work to achieve this by understanding the importance of affirmed names and pronouns and respecting others' identities.

title: The Importance of Pronouns
content:
### **How Do I Ask About Pronouns?**

**Individual interactions:**

Introduce yourself and share your pronouns first: “Hi, my name is Rachel and I use she/her pronouns. What is your name and what pronouns do you use?

**Group interactions:**

During a first meeting or class session, it is best practice to allow folks to introduce themselves, sharing their affirmed name and pronouns: “let’s go around and say our name, pronouns and major.”

Another best practice is getting in the habit of asking for everyone's affirmed name and pronouns, not just those we assume are trans/non-binary.

Asking for folks' name and pronouns every now and then is good practice as identity is fluid and can change over time. This is also related to our campus commitment to inclusive excellence.

title: The Importance of Pronouns
content:
### **Implementing Pronouns**

Implementing your pronouns can be done in the following ways:

* When you do individual and group introductions
* On your name tags
* On your email signatures
* Adding them to your Canvas profile
* Adding them to your Zoom profile

**Gender-affirming Care**

* We do not offer HRT or the continuation of it. However, due to a reciprocity agreement among CSU campuses, as a CPP student, you can access basic health services at any other CSU campus at no additional charge, which may include gender-affirming services. Here are some nearby CSU campuses that offer gender-affirming care:
  + **CSU Long Beach**
  + **CSU LA**
  + **CSU Fullerton**
* [Planned Parenthood](https://www.plannedparenthood.org/get-care/our-services/transgender-hormone-therapy) continues to be a great resource for folks and is another option for gender-affirming care.
* [Use this link to find a Queer friendly doctor.](https://www.lgbtqcenteroc.org/linkage-to-care/) Courtesy of the OC LGBTQ Center.

**Gender Inclusive Restrooms**

* Across from the Pride Center is an All-Gender Multi-Stall Restroom in Building 26 (Old Stables)
* Please see the full list of restrooms[HERE](https://www.cpp.edu/officeofequity/gender-identity/list-gir.shtml)

**Affirmed Name**

* If you are looking to update your gender and name on the official records system so that they appear on your transcript, payroll records, and financial aid documents, you're going to need to submit a Change of Information form to the registrar's office. [Here is a link to that form.](https://www.cpp.edu/registrar/forms/change-of-information-form.pdf)
* For most official registrar changes, you will need proof of legal identity change such as a government-issued identification card, marriage license, divorce decree, or other court document.
* However, there are quite a few places where you don't need official documents of a legal name change. [Here is the link to access this.](https://www.cpp.edu/registrar/student-record/update-personal-information.shtml)
* Students affirmed name may be used for class rosters, appear on BroncoDirect, online Directory Information, Canvas, student identification card, and name on the student’s email. It may also be disclosed as directory information unless the student declines to permit such disclosure.
* For more information, please reach out to the Pride Center Coordinator at pride\_center@cpp.edu

title: Gender Inclusive Restrooms
content:
## On Campus

Having access to a comfortable and safe public restroom should not be a luxury. Transgender and gender nonconforming/gender expansive people often face the burden of being confronted or questioned about which restroom they should use. The Pride Center, Disability Resource Center and Women’s Resource Center are dedicated to documenting the places on campus where students, staff and faculty can use a single or multi-stall gender-inclusive restroom. title: Womxn's Resource Center
content:
# Womxn's Resource Center

![Students utilize the computers inside the WRC](img/24-wrc-students.jpg)

title: Womxn's Resource Center
content:
## Mission Statement

The Womxn’s Resource Center promotes gender equity through empowerment and consciousness raising, and strives to establish a brave space which encourages awareness and discussion of womxn’s issues.

The WRC warmly welcomes any person, regardless of how they identify. It is also a space for people of all backgrounds to explore, learn, connect and build coalition.

We celebrate diversity in all its forms and are committed to providing a respectful and inclusive environment for every individual.

title: Womxn's Resource Center
content:
### What Services Does the WRC Provide?

The Womxn's Resource Center provides programs, workshops, referrals, group support and opportunities for personal and academic growth. The WRC creates an environment where womxn-identified individuals can flourish while advocating for a greater understanding of the impact of gender in our institution and in our society. We have five main focuses to our services, which include:

**Community**

Our center aims to provide opportunities for students to meet new people and make new friends. Creating a community of support through positive one on one interactions, intentional mentoring programs, and volunteer opportunities is one of our top priorities!

**Support**

The Womxn's Resource Center creates a community of support by having space that encourages the exchange of advice, connects students to needed resources, and provides staff to just listen. Our peer circles encourage our community of support, along with one on one advising from our full-time coordinator, and our self-care happy hours.

**Resources**

Our physical space provides our community with access to a range of resources. These include: a gender inclusive restroom, a lounge for hanging out and studying, a computer lab, free tampons, pads, and condoms, a fridge, a microwave, and a lactation space. These are open to all students to utilize while our center is open.

**Identity Development**

We provide programs and conversations that encourage the development of the womxn identity. Catch us during our Womxn's Leadership Institute discussing the socialization of womxn identities or tune in to some of our favorite podcasts in the center that talk about intersecting identities.

**Education**

We hope to raise the consciousness of our Cal Poly Pomona community through education opportunities. We host Womxn Wednesday Workshops once per month that take a topic or issue in society and frame it through the lens of being a womxn.
section: What Services Does the WRC Provide?

title: Womxn's Resource Center
content:
### Why Do We Spell Womxn With an 'X'?

“Womxn” is one of a few lexical and social phenomena, including the adoption of “x” in naming gender nonconforming individuals, genderqueer folxs, two spirit people, femmes, transgender folxs, and non-binary people. This term recognizes the history of feminism has included racism, transphobia and gender binary views. The WRC and our space are open to all and support all the experiences of the womxn in our community.

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content: # Womxn's Leadership Institute

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content:
## About the Institute

The Womxn's Leadership Institute is a 1-day in-person leadership development conference focused on empowering womxn students at CPP through community building, mentorship, networking and workshops facilitated by industrious womxn leaders.

Our goal is to foster womxn's leadership and mentorship, and **all Cal Poly Pomona students** interested in furthering this mission may participate in the Institute, regardless of their race, color, national origin or sex.

![Students at the WLI](../img/wrc5.jpg)

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content: ## Frequently Asked Questions

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content:
### What is the Womxn's Leadership Institute?

The Womxn's Leadership Institute is a 1-day in-person leadership development conference focused on empowering students at Cal Poly Pomona through community building, mentorship, networking and workshops facilitated by industrious womxn leaders.
section: What is the Womxn's Leadership Institute?

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content:
### Will Meals be Provided?

Breakfast, lunch, dinner, and snacks will be provided.

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content:
### What Are the Attendee Requirements?

* Must successfully complete the [sign-up form](https://tinyurl.com/WLI23) and be the first 75 to attend
* Must be a Cal Poly Pomona student
* Must be available for the entirety of the institute

title: Womxn's Leadership Institute
content:
### I Am a Faculty/Staff Member. How Can I Support?

Facilitators are trusted womxn leaders and role models who lead small cohorts of WLI student participants throughout the event, help answer questions and concerns, and facilitate open dialogues and activities among their cohort. We intentionally match facilitators with their cohort group based on shared lived experiences so that our student participants feel seen, heard, valued, as well see someone like themselves in in a leadership role.

***Thank you for your interest -- submissions are now closed.***

Volunteers support the WLI committee with tasks before and during the event. All folx, regardless of gender identity, are encouraged to volunteer.
section: I Am a Faculty/Staff Member. How Can I Support?

title: Veterans Resource Center
content:
### Welcome, Military Affiliated Students!

Cal Poly Pomona is grateful for the contributions made by members of the United States Armed Services, both at home and abroad. As a military-friendly campus, we offer a dynamic and supportive academic community and hands-on opportunities for your leadership, academic and personal growth.

[Read More About the VRC](about-us.shtml)

content:
## Contact Us

Monday - Friday from 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

**Location:**SSB (121-W) 1st Floor, Office 1940

**Phone:**(909) 869-6994

**Email:**vetquestions@cpp.edu

title: Veterans Resource Center
content:
## Get Started

[### Veterans Admission

Cal Poly Pomona gives high priority for admission and enrollment to veterans in recognition of their service to the country. Learn more about admission requirements, military credit and more.](admissions.shtml)

[### Veterans Enrollment

Veterans bring to the classroom the skills they have acquired through their military training and experiences. As a veteran, you may have unique needs that we are prepared to support as you pursue your college degree. Learn more about orientation, priority registration, call to military service and more.](enrollment.shtml)

[### Money Matters

Your college education is one of the biggest, most important investments you and your family will make. You may be wondering where to start in making the right decision. Learn more about your student account, education costs, tuition and fees, veteran benefits, financial aid, scholarships and student jobs.](money-matters.shtml)

[### Student Support Services

The university offers several programs and services to meet the unique academic and personal needs of our veterans, including the VRC as a central hub of information and support. Learn more about student services and other services available to veterans.](support-services.shtml)

[### Faculty and Staff Resources

We're committed to providing faculty and staff useful resources and information that can assist them in supporting the success of our student veterans. Learn more about academic support, campus liaisons, workshops, presentations and publications and the Veterans Success Committee.](faculty-staff.shtml)

[### News and Events

Learn more about what programs, services and activities the Veterans Resource Center has available for CPP students by checking out the VRC's semester SITREP newsletter and calendar of events.](news-and-events.shtml)

[### About Us

The Veterans Resource Center provides veterans and military personnel a centralized source for information, support, guidance and community. Learn more about the VRC, hours, location and contact information.](about-us.shtml)

[### Giving

Gifts to the Veterans Resource Center make the support services offered possible. Learn about our donors and how to donate.](giving.shtml)

title: About Us
content:
## About the VRC

The Veterans Resource Center was established in 2012 to support the personal and academic success of Cal Poly Pomona's military affiliated students. The Center serves as a central resource for information, support, guidance and community. Among some of the services that are offered to our student veterans and dependents are:

* Introduction to veteran-specific services
* Computers and free printing
* Referrals to campus and off-campus programs and services
* Peer support, counseling and guidance
* Assistance with navigating policies and processes
* Connection with other veterans
* Advocacy and support with campus faculty, staff, and administrators
* Scholarship opportunities
* Career workshops and events
* Veteran recognition events

By establishing a connection with the Center, veterans and dependents provide themselves with an opportunity to benefit from the resources and support services that the University and our off campus partners have made available to them to ensure their well-being and success as students.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content: # Academic Related Support for Veterans

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## Academic Related Support for Veterans

Veterans and military personnel may come to you for assistance with situations or needs that are unique to their military status.  Here is a brief summary of some of the requests you may receive:

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Educational Plan Requirement for VA Benefits**

To get VA educational benefits certified, student veterans (and eligible dependents of veterans) must complete and submit an Academic Year [Educational Plan (PDF)](https://www.cpp.edu/registrar//forms/va-educational-plan.pdf)  that needs to be approved by an academic advisor.  This plan is then submitted by the Certifying Official in the Registrar’s Office to the Veterans Administration.

It is important to note that VA educational benefits   only  cover courses that meet a degree requirement (GE, core, support or unrestricted elective). Due to the limited time frame in which benefits are available, student veterans are usually very focused on getting into classes that can be counted towards their degree completion and may request assistance in enrolling into a specific course.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Call to Military Service**

Members of the Armed Forces, National Guard or Reserve may be called to active duty.  These students are instructed to notify the Veterans Resource Center at vrcbenefits@cpp.edu, unless notice is precluded by military necessity.

Student veterans are entitled to readmission to the University if their separation is caused by either a voluntary or involuntary active duty service for a period of more than 30 days.

To resume studies after completion of their military service, students must notify their Certifying Official in the Registrar’s Office or contact the Veterans Resource Center. They will be advised to schedule a meeting with their advisor to establish an academic year educational plan for the completion of their degree.

More information about [military leave of absence (Planned Educational Leave)](https://catalog.cpp.edu/content.php?catoid=69&navoid=5889#military_leave_of_absence) is available on the University Catalog. To submit a Leave of Absence visit the [Registrar's Office](https://www.cpp.edu/registrar/forms/forms.shtml) website.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Short-Term Military Service**

Military personnel called to *mandatory training*  may or may not know of the training schedule in advance.  In cases where the training conflicts with classes, exams, or assignments instructors may be asked for an accommodation or schedule adjustment.

For military leave that is less than 30 days, review the [Accommodations of Military Student Service Obligations (Less than 30 Days) policy](https://catalog.cpp.edu/content.php?catoid=69&navoid=5889#military_leave_of_absence#military_leave_of_absence)  on the university catalog for more information.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Military Transfer Credit**

Student veterans are eligible for elective credit and GE Area E if they meet certain requirements. Students may also petition for an evaluation of their military transcript for course substitution or transfer coursework credit.

Unless the course(s) have been previously articulated, academic departments and advisors are required to review and, if applicable, approve these petitions based on the ACE Guide to the Evaluation of Educational Experiences in the Armed Services.  More information about transfer credit for veterans is available at the   [Transfer Credit Guide for Veterans (Semester) PDF](https://www.cpp.edu/~registrar/files/public/transfercreditguideforveteranssemester.pdf) or at [Transfer Guide for Veterans (Quarter) PDF.](https://www.cpp.edu/~registrar/files/public/TransferCreditGuideforVeterans.pdf)

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Class absence for VA medical appointments**

In some cases, medical appointments with the Veterans Administration have to be made several months in advance and may conflict with a veteran’s class or exam schedule.  Student veterans may request that an assignment or exam time be adjusted around the appointment with the Veterans Administration.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## **Accommodations for veterans with disabilities**

Student veterans may require accommodations or assistance on the basis of a disability through the Disability Resource Center.   For questions about accommodations for veterans with disabilities, please contact the Disability Resource Center (DRC) at 909-869-3333.

title: Academic Related Support for Veterans
content:
## For More Information...

We encourage you to contact the Veterans Resource Center for information and/or assistance with providing veterans with the support they need to meet their academic goals. For more information, you may call 909-869-6994.

title: Academic Support
content:
## Reach Out for Support

Achieving your graduation goal will include challenges that will help you develop your critical thinking and problem-solving skills. These challenges can be best met with the support of faculty, advisors and learning support staff who are committed to your academic and personal success.

You may face a number of challenging situations, including building your class schedule and academic plan, passing a difficult course, or trying to move your grade from a B to an A.

Cal Poly Pomona offers a comprehensive [Student Success Central](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/)page that provides online support and points you to other help resources such as the [Bronco Advising Center](https://www.cpp.edu/studentsuccess/bronco-advising-center/index.shtml). Information about additional academic support options is available at [Academic & Support Resources](http://www.cpp.edu/~academics/academic-resources/index.shtml).

Reach out for academic help early and often.

title: Admission Requirements for Veterans
content: # Admission Requirements for Veterans

title: Admission Requirements for Veterans
content:
## Requirements

The California State University system and specifically Cal Poly Pomona give veterans and military service members the highest consideration for admission and enrollment in recognition of their service in the U.S. Armed Forces.

**Admission Requirements**

Admission requirements and deadlines are based on whether you are applying as a first-time freshman, undergraduate transfer, graduate or credential student. Go to [Admissions](https://www.cpp.edu/admissions/) and review the material applicable to your level of study.

Recognizing that veterans may not be able to meet the standard admission application filing deadlines and/or mandatory orientation requirements due to service or travel limitations, Cal Poly Pomona offers veterans additional flexibility throughout the admissions, orientation and registration processes if needed. Admission applications from veterans are given priority consideration and are reviewed on a case-by-case basis since every veteran’s academic and military experience may be different.

If you have difficulty completing the admission process within the admission application period, contact the Veterans Resource Center at (909) 869-6994 or at vetquestions@cpp.edu to discuss possible alternatives.

**Pre-admission Advising**

We recommend that you set up a pre-advising appointment with an outreach counselor early in the admission process to help facilitate your admission and enrollment at Cal Poly Pomona.  To schedule an appointment visit the Office of Outreach, Recruitment, & Educational Partnerships' [pre-admission advising](https://www.cpp.edu/outreach/pre-admission-advising.shtml) web page.

California State University, Pomona prohibits any fraudulent, aggressive and/or high pressure recruitment practices directed specifically toward military students.
