title: Advisory Committee
content:
### Nancy Harvey

![Nancy Harvey](../img/about/advisory-committee/nancy-harvey.jpg)Nancy Harvey is the immediate past president of the Arabian Horse Association and has a long history of being a USEF Licensed Official. With 40+ years of dedication to the equine industry, Nancy has acquired a wealth of knowledge through various experiences as an amateur, trainer, exhibitor, recreational rider, show secretary and show manager. Nancy has utilized her knowledge and experience through advocation for the Arabian, along with other breeds as well, by volunteering her time with numerous foundations, associations, and other organizations.

title: Advisory Committee
content:
### Paula Hitzler, MSU Horse Teaching and Research

![Paula Hitzler](../img/about/advisory-committee/paula-hitzler.jpg)Paula Hitzler’s career with horses spans nearly 45 years.  Paula entered the horse world at the age of nine when she began showing her family’s Arabian horses.  By the time Paula turned 18, she had numerous regional and national titles under her belt.  Paula then went on to attend Michigan State University and enroll in its Animal Science program.  Upon graduating from MSU, Paula left Michigan to manage Dallas-based Zodiac Farm’s breeding operation.  From Texas, Paula went on to Sonoma, California, where she managed Saddle Rock Ranch.  It was also at that time when she began her teaching career.  Paula taught Horse Management courses at California’s San Joaquin Delta College and Santa Rosa Junior College.

In 1989, Paula was asked by her alma mater to return to Michigan to manage Michigan State University’s Horse Teaching and Research Center.  It was a perfect match – she never left and continues as its manager.  Paula teaches many of the Institute of Agricultural Technology Horse Management courses offered by MSU’s Department of Animal Science.  These courses include starting horses under saddle, advanced horsemanship, introduction to horse science lab, young horse handling and labs in reproduction and breeding farm management.  Paula’s proudest moments have occurred when the MSU students she has taught have shown MSU bred and trained horses to numerous regional and national titles.
section: Paula Hitzler, MSU Horse Teaching and Research

title: Advisory Committee
content:
### Janina Merz and Om El Arab

![Janina Merz and om El Arab](../img/about/advisory-committee/janina-merz.jpg)Janina Merz is a 4th generation horse breeder and the owner of Om El Arab. Janina’s mother, the late Sigi Siller, founded the farm in 1970, in Germany’s Black Forest region. In 1984, with the incomparable success of Om El Arab-bred horses in Europe, the family decided to expand their breeding operation to the Santa Ynez Valley, in California, where it is located today. Om El Arab has full-service breeding capabilities, including ultrasounds, embryo transfers, collecting, cooling, and shipping semen, with supporting facilities for foaling and herd management. These operations are combined with training, showing, promotion, education, riding, and sales. Over the past 52 years, Om El Arab has become one of the most successful private Arabian horse breeding programs, with champion horses worldwide carrying their bloodlines. Janina worked alongside her mom since 1998, and took over sole ownership and management of Om El Arab in 2016. She is the current Secretary for the Santa Ynez Valley Arabian Horse Club, and has served in various Board of Director positions for the club. She is also on the Board of Directors of the Arabian Breeders World Cup.
section: Janina Merz and Om El Arab

title: Advisory Committee
content:
### John Resich '69, Green Hills Memorial Park

![John Resich](../img/about/advisory-committee/john-resich.jpg)John J. Resich Jr. is an attorney and rancher and serves as chairman of the board and vice president of development of Green Hills Memorial Park, where he has designed, constructed and overseen the development of 17 projects over the last 15 years. He provides legal representation to cemeteries throughout California, gives legal advice concerning legislation and has testified as an expert witness for the cemetery industry. Resich owns ranching and farming operations in Wyoming, raising cattle and crops including corn, beans, alfalfa and grass. He earned his law degree at Santa Clara University in 1972. He received his master’s degree in business at Chapman University in 2001. Resich founded the Marine Mammal Care Center, which rescues marine mammals and releases them back into the environment after they are treated, and is a former founding member of the San Pedro Peninsula Hospital Foundation. He also is a board member of the [Cal Poly Pomona Philanthropic Foundation](https://www.cpp.edu/agri/about/board-of-advisors.shtml) and the Huntley College Board of Advisors.
section: John Resich '69, Green Hills Memorial Park

title: Advisory Committee
content:
### Margaret Rich, Green Acres Ranch

![Margaret Rich](../img/about/advisory-committee/margaret-rich.jpg)Margaret West Rich, grew up with Arabian horses. Her father and mother purchased the family’s first Arabian horses from several foundation breeders located in Southern California. The most influential were Herbert H. Reese and Gladys Brown Edwards. At the time Mr. Reese was overseeing the Kellogg Arabian horses located where Cal Poly Pomona stands today and Gladys was working as Mr. Reese’s secretary. After graduation from UC Berkley Margaret took on the responsibility of running Green Acres Ranch. She brought the ranch and its horses into the 21st century.

Today, Green Acres introduces hundreds of individuals yearly to the Arabian horse through its very large lesson program that uses primarily Arabian horses and a few other breeds.

GAIT (Green Acres Interactive Therapy) is a therapeutic riding program that Margaret founded over 30 years ago. GAIT is located at Green Acres Ranch and also embraces Arabian horses in its lessons.

Margaret is a licensed Arabian horse judge who served over 20 years on the Education and Evaluation Commission. She also served two terms on the BOD of the Arabian Horse Association as Director from Region 1.

Margaret’s knowledge and expertise in the Horse industry benefits countless individuals. She has helped guide and shape the future of our horse community as not just a professional horsewoman but as a community leader. She was most recently recognized as 2021 Woman of the Year for the State of California District 28.

Margaret has served as a member of the Cal Poly Pomona President’s Arabian Horse Advisory Committee since 2012.

content:

title: Facility
content:
## W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center

The W. K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is a showcase facility that houses approximately 100 purebred Arabian horses used in teaching, research, and breeding. The Center includes 40 acres of irrigated pasture, five barns for stabling horses, foaling stalls with an observation room, veterinary sciences laboratory, several arenas, a farrier shop, and research facilities.

![Arabians graze with snowy mountain background](../img/about/facility-gallery/23horses-and-mtns.png)

The Arabian Horse Center breeding program welcomes numerous foals per year and offers horses for sale to the general public. A sales list is available on the Center website, listing breeding, history, and prices. Cal Poly Pomona horses are exhibited by students and the horse center’s trainers in competitive all-Arabian horse shows, the Pasadena Tournament of Roses Parade, national Arabian horse shows, California Arabian horse shows, and Cal Poly Pomona Sunday horse shows. The hundreds of ribbons and trophies displayed at the Center attest to the quality of the Arabians at the W. K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center.

title: Facility
content:
## Weanling Barn

Located next to the hot walker, the Weanling Barn contains more box stalls, and also houses the equine treadmill, breeding room, well-equipped equine veterinary clinic complete with breeding stocks, and small animal clinic. Both staff and visiting veterinarians make regular use of the equine clinic area, and the small animal clinic is the perfect space for the college’s Animal Science Department lab classes.

content:
## Mare Barn

Built in the style of a mare motel, with eight large partially covered paddocks and a wide, covered center aisle, the Mare Barn also holds the farrier lab and the spacious farm shop at its far end. At the front of the mare barn is an equine scale that is regularly used by students and staff.

title: Facility
content:
## Pastures & Paddocks

The AHC is situated on 38 acres, leaving plenty of space for pastures and paddocks. Pastures are on both an irrigation and a rotation schedule, keeping them lush and green for the groups of horses that occupy them. The center’s young horses are divided into age groups and spend the first several years of their life living outside in small herds. Several smaller grass paddocks are located on either side of the Main Barn and are used for turnout on a regular basis.

title: The Kellogg Legacy
content:
# The Kellogg Legacy

![W.K. Kellogg and two Foals](../img/about/kellogg-and-foals2.png)

> Learn the history of the Kellogg ranch at W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Library.

[Visit W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Library](https://libguides.library.cpp.edu/wkkahl%20)

title: The Kellogg Legacy
content:
## The Kellogg Legacy

The W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center was established in 1925 by the cereal magnate Will Keith Kellogg of Battle Creek, Michigan. In his quest to fulfill a childhood dream, W.K. Kellogg sought out the finest Arabian bloodlines of the day to begin a breeding program at his winter home in Pomona, California. He acquired horses from within the United States and abroad, importing several horses from Lady Wentworth’s famed Crabbet Arabian Stud in England.

![W.K. Kellogg and an Arabian Horse](../img/legacy/legacy-kellogg-and-horse.png)The ranch became a popular destination for 1920s Hollywood stars to visit rare Arabian horses, and Kellogg’s horses appeared in several films. The ranch became so popular that a show was established on Sundays in order to better showcase the Arabian horses.

W.K. Kellogg presented the ranch to the state of California in 1932 with the stipulation that the Arabian breeding program and the Sunday Shows be maintained.

During World War II, use of the ranch was given to the U.S. Army as a facility for breeding war horses. The Kellogg Ranch became the Pomona Quartermaster Depot in 1943. One of the most notable events of the army days was the acquisition of the Polish Arabian horses that were rescued ahead of the advance of the Russian army at the end of the war. Twenty-one Polish Arabians, including the stallion \*Witez II, were brought to the ranch in 1946.

The U.S. Army ceased breeding horses in 1948, and the Ranch was transferred into the ownership of the U.S. Department of Agriculture. During this time, much of the ranch’s best stock was dispersed around the country. The future of the ranch seemed uncertain, and envoys from both the Kellogg Foundation and the Kellogg Company went to Washington to lobby for its continuation.

![W.K. Kellogg and friends.](../img/legacy/legacy-kellogg-and-men.png)The ranch was turned over to California State Polytechnic College San Luis Obispo as their southern branch in 1949. This new venture by Cal Poly joined forces with the Voorhis School, a men’s agricultural college, which was located in nearby San Dimas. All instructional programs were moved to the present campus in 1956. Cal Poly Voorhis-Kellogg Campus admitted its first female students in 1961. The newly co-educational college separated from Cal Poly San Luis Obispo in 1966, and attendance had grown large enough by 1972 for Cal Poly Pomona to be granted University status. Today, Cal Poly Pomona has over 26,000 students in eight different academic colleges.

The W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center’s current breeding philosophy was established in 1964 when Norman K. Dunn was appointed as Manager of the Horse Department. Professor Dunn surmised that the Arabian breed was surpassing the insular breeding program at Cal Poly, and proposed that Cal Poly mares be bred to outside stallions. Some of the first outside stallions suggested for breeding to Cal Poly mares were \*Bajram, \*Bask, Fadjur, and The Real McCoy. The breeding program continues in this tradition today, breeding to the top stallions from all over the United States.

True to W.K. Kellogg’s wishes, the horses are still presented in the Sunday Shows, at 2:00 pm on the first Sunday of every month, October-May. Per the conditions of the deed, the President's Arabian Advisory Committee meets semi-annually. The members of the committee and recent minutes can be found in the  [advisory committee](advisory-committee.shtml) page.

To learn more about the history of the Kellogg ranch, please contact the   [W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Library](https://libguides.library.cpp.edu/wkkahl).

title: Ways to Give
content:
# Ways to Give

![W.K. Kellogg ](../img/giving/w.k.kellogg-portrait.png)

> “Education offers the greatest opportunity for really improving one generation over another.”

W.K. Kellogg

[Give Now](cpp.shtml)

We hope you will find this site helpful as you learn more about supporting the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center.

* **Cash Gifts** can be made through credit/debit card transactions, outright cash, or personal checks, cashier’s checks, wire transfers, or money orders.
* **Pledges** enable you to plan a gift that is both convenient and tax-wise. A pledge allows you to consider a more significant gift that otherwise may not be possible and is payable over five years.
* **Gifts of Securities** may be contributed as outright gifts or as a pledge payment.  When you make a gift of long-term appreciated securities (stocks, bonds or mutual funds that have grown in value), your income tax deduction is equal to the current fair market value of the securities, and you may avoid capital gains taxes on the transfer.
* **Donor Advised Fund (DAF)** is a charitable giving vehicle that allows you to make a tax-deductible donation today and then recommend gifts to the charity over time. Not only do contributions have immediate tax benefits, but they also grow tax-free in the account to provide greater impact.
* **Donor Advised Fund Cal Poly Pomona (DAF – CPP)** is like a foundation or charitable savings account and gives you the flexibility to recommend how much and how often money is granted to your favorite program at Cal Poly Pomona. When you make a gift to us of cash or other assets, we take your tax-deductible contribution and establish a special account in your name. Your account is then invested to grow over time, permitting you to recommend annual gifts to the university. Simply contact us for an application form to complete, and we will get you started!
* **Gifts of Real Estate** can be contributed as an outright gift or a gift through your estate. It also may be given to fund a deferred gift such as a charitable remainder trust. Other gifting options allow for continued use of the property during your lifetime or for lifetime income.
* **Gifts-in-Kind** are gifts of tangible personal property such as equipment, materials, crops, and company products or inventory.
* **Matching Gifts** enable you to double or triple the impact of your outright gifts if your employer offers a matching gift program. To determine whether your or your spouse’s company will match your gift, please visit the [Matching Gifts](https://www.cpp.edu/giving/give-to-cpp/matching-gifts.shtml) page.
* **Honorary or Memorial Funds** are created to make contributions in honor or memory of someone special to you. You can create a fund in their memory or contribute to one that is already set up.
* **Deferred Gifts** enable you to arrange charitable contributions in a manner that maximizes your personal objectives and financial goals. Many plans provide flexibility during your lifetime, even though the college will not realize the benefit until sometime in the future. The most common types of deferred plans are bequests, retirement plan designations, charitable remainder trusts, charitable lead trusts, and gifts of paid-up life insurance policies. For more information on deferred giving opportunities and the Kellogg Voorhis Society, please visit the [Planned Giving](https://cpp.giftlegacy.com/) page.

To learn more about these initiatives and how you can support them, please [contact us](https://www.cpp.edu/wkkelloggarabianhorsecenter/about/contact.shtml) or [make an online gift](https://www.cpp.edu/wkkelloggarabianhorsecenter/giving/cpp.shtml).

title: Why Give
content:
# Why Give

In 1925, cereal magnate Will Keith Kellogg purchased a 377-acre ranch in Southern California’s Pomona Valley, with the intention of breeding the finest Arabians in North America.

Kellogg was a promoter of the Arabian—a light horse breed, cherished for its intelligence, versatility, endurance, and beauty—to the American public. In 1926, he began Sunday Shows, attracting large crowds and Hollywood celebrities. The horses also were featured in popular Hollywood films and movie star publicity photos.

Based on the success of Kellogg's Arabian Horse Ranch, he continued to expand and ultimately donated 813 acres to the State of California. Over time, the property was transferred to the California State Polytechnic College, Kellogg-Voorhis, then a part of Cal Poly San Luis Obispo. Cal Poly Pomona became an independent campus in 1966.

Today the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is a breeding industry leader, promoting the beauty and characteristics of this majestic animal. The Center is committed to advancing education and research using the Arabian horses that are known for their high intelligence, willingness, courage, curiosity, and sociality. This program continues the legacy of W.K. Kellogg and is a commitment to Cal Poly Pomona’s “learn-by-doing” philosophy.

Investment in the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center enriches the experience for every participant and visitor. Your gift to support any of the following programs is the single most direct way you can invest in the future of the Arabian Horse:

* **Excellence Funds** or unrestricted resources for the center’s greatest needs.
* **Scholarships** create opportunities for students from all walks of life to reach their full potential. By supporting scholarships, you ensure the best students can participate in learning about the Arabian horse, no matter their financial circumstances.
* **Program Support** enables students to explore their passions both in and out of the classroom to maximize their Cal Poly Pomona experience. A variety of services support student success such as Horses for Heroes, Sunday Shows, Horsemanship Club, and Student Assistants.
* **Facilities and Equipment** play a vital role in meeting our educational mission. We continually work to improve and strengthen our existing infrastructure and facilities to provide improved teaching, learning, and outreach.
* **Lasting Legacy** or endowed funds provide support in perpetuity. The initial gift is invested, and funds are awarded from the investment income, preserving the principal so funds continue to generate income in future years.

To learn more about these initiatives and how you can support them, please contact us or [make an online gift](https://www.cpp.edu/wkkelloggarabianhorsecenter/giving/cpp.shtml).

title: Foal Watch
content: ## Foal Watch – AVS 2434 (1 unit)

title: Foal Watch
content:
### Spring Semester Course

![Arabian Oakley and her foal.](../img/student-information/22-arabian-oakley7.png)

This in-person lecture course provides students the opportunity to learn information regarding basic equine breeding management, care of the pregnant mare, methods to detect impending foaling and parturition, difficulties that might occur during foaling, post-foaling care and potential problems/disorders in foals.

Topics for this course include the following:

* Breeding the Mare and Choosing the Stallion
* Fetal Development, placenta and position in utero
* Preparing for the foal: housing, monitoring mare and building a foaling kit
* Stages of Labor: Stage I - Foal position for birthing/Mare behavior, Stage II – Foal expelling and Stage III Placenta delivery
* Normal Foaling and Dystocia
* Post-partum behaviors in the mare
* The first 12 hours of a foal’s life
* Congenital and Genetic Disorders in the foal
* Rejected and Orphaned Foals
* Foal Behavior from birth to 6 months of age
* Basic handling and halter training a newborn foal
* Health checks for foal and types of weaning practices

Each week a new topic is introduced, the course is offered to any student enrolled at the University and is offered every Spring Semester, Wednesday evening from 5:00 to 5:50. Students have the opportunity to watch the mares deliver their foals via live video feed and also suggest registered names for the new foals. There are ‘optional’ opportunities to observe the handling of the foals after they are born, which includes halter training, leading, and learning the normal ‘hands-on’ aspects of foal health care.

Foal Watch is offered spring semester.

Interested students please call [Animal and Veterinary Sciences](https://www.cpp.edu/agri/animal-science/index.shtml), 909-869-2216, to see when courses are available.

title: Horses for Heroes
content:
# Horses for Heroes

![Horses for Heroes participants and their Arabian horses.](../img/student-information/22horses-for-heroes3.png)

In partnership with the Cal Poly Pomona Veterans Resource Center (VRC), the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is a proud partner with the Horses for Heroes program.

In the spirit of a polytechnic education or 'learn by doing', the program pairs current student veterans with graduate assistants to groom and care for horses. The program also includes training in equine behavior, horse handling, and horse health.

Starting in Fall 2019, the program is based on Cal Poly Pomona’s very successful Foal Watch program. Although Horses for Heroes is not a therapeutic program for PTSD, it does include training on coping skills and elements from similar programs. No previous horse or ranch experience is necessary to participate.

”It gives veterans the opportunity to focus on the present and also on their well-being,” says Elke Azpeitia, Director of Cal Poly Pomona’s [Veterans Resource Center](https://www.cpp.edu/veterans/index.shtml).

title: IHSA Horse Show Team
content: # IHSA Horse Show Team

title: IHSA Horse Show Team
content:
## IHSA Horse Show Team

![A group of Sunday Show performers in costumes with their Arabian Horses.](horse-power.png)

With over 350 colleges and more than 8,400 riders, the [Intercollegiate Horse Shows Association (IHSA)](https://www.ihsainc.com/) is the world's largest Intercollegiate Equestrian organization. Cal Poly Pomona is a member of IHSA Zone 8 Region 2 (southern California & Arizona) and competes in IHSA shows around the southern California region. We typically attend 8-10 Hunt Seat shows and 6-9 Western shows throughout the regular competition season. Riders compete all year long, accumulating points as individuals and also for our team. After the regular season is complete, top individuals and teams can advance to the Zone or Semi Final level, and then IHSA Nationals.

Riders may compete in Hunter Seat and/or Western Equitation with levels from Walk/Trot through Open Equitation over Fences, or Walk/Jog through Western Horsemanship/Reining classes. The team is largely run by students, with our more experienced riders running the weekly group lessons. Throughout the year, professional trainers and coaches conduct clinics at our facility or theirs, to supplement the instruction that riders receive.

Anybody can join the [Horse Show Team](https://cppequestrian.wixsite.com/cppequestrian?fbclid=PAAaYy2U9LDk3qxCUY2kO0Yf-XtQD7fxK7J_MafgYF9SbDy_Cu38bYCBtpBLU); owning a horse is not required. The team owns several horses which are used for practices and competitions. Any level of riding experience is accepted.

Team Orientation is held at the end of September at the Horse Center, and meetings are held every Tuesday during the school year in Building 2, Room 112 at noon.

title: IHSA Horse Show Team
content:
## About Our Shows

The Cal Poly Pomona Horse Show Team competes as part of the Intercollegiate Horse Show Association. This organization levels out the playing field in a sport where money has so much to do with success in the show ring. Instead of being judged on which rider has the nicest horse, the classes are judged on who can ride what they are given with the best position. Shows are hosted by various colleges in the region, and all the colleges in the region try to pool horses together for the shows. The morning of the show, those who are competing are not permitted to ride at all before their class. They watch all the horses in the competition warm up with designated warm-up riders, and then go to a draw table to see which horse they will be riding in their classes. Sometimes riders luck out and draw a horse they know quite well, and other times, they draw a horse they've never laid their eyes on. This keeps riders on their toes and means they must be adaptable riders, as no two horses are the same.

title: IHSA Horse Show Team
content:
## Team Horses

Cal Poly Pomona is proud to own and lease some amazing horses! They come from diverse backgrounds and make great mounts and teachers for beginning and advanced riders alike. We would like to thank those who have donated the use and ownership of their horses for our lesson program and shows. For more information about horse donation or leasing, please contact cppequestrian@gmail.com.

content: # Scholarships and Financial Aid

title: Scholarships and Financial Aid
content:
## Scholarships and Financial Aid

At Cal Poly Pomona, we are committed to creating opportunities for students from all walks of life to reach their full potential. Our scholarship program ensures that the best students can attend and thrive at the university, no matter their financial circumstances.

Thanks to the generosity of numerous donors, we offer both merit- and need-based scholarships for the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center.

title: Scholarships and Financial Aid
content:
## Scholarship Information for Current Students

Apply through the [Bronco Scholarship Application (BSA)](https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/scholarships/cal-poly-pomona-scholarships.shtml). Scholarships will be awarded by the end of the spring quarter. Students must be enrolled in a minimum of 12 units each quarter.

title: Scholarships and Financial Aid
content:
## Scholarship Information for Prospective Students

Cal Poly Pomona offers university-wide scholarships for incoming and continuing students and provides a database of other scholarship resources. Please visit the [Office of Financial Aid and Scholarships](https://www.cpp.edu/financial-aid/scholarships/index.shtml) for more information.

title: Scholarships and Financial Aid
content:
## W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center-Based Scholarships

Through the support of loyal alumni and donors, the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is proud to offer several scholarships based on academic interest. While these scholarships vary in the award range, the university is committed to every student having the opportunity to pursue an exceptional and affordable education at Cal Poly Pomona. W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center-based scholarships include:

title: Student Employment
content:
## Stall Cleaners

The W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center employs several students as stall cleaners. Each stall cleaner is assigned a set of stalls to maintain. These stalls may be cleaned at the student’s convenience during normal business hours. If you are interested in a position as a stall cleaner, please email arabianhorse@cpp.edu or call (909) 869-4988.

title: Student Employment
content:
## Grooms

The training staff of the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center employ a select number of students as grooms. Student grooms perform all of the tasks necessary to keep the training barn running. In order to be qualified for a groom position, a student should be comfortable with handling Arabian show horses, clipping and bathing, tacking, and performing simple ground work. Students support special exhibitions. Grooms are paid for up to 20 hours of work per week and minimum academic standards must be maintained. Positions are limited. If you are interested in a position as a groom, please email arabianhorse@cpp.edu or call (909) 869-4988.

title: Student Employment
content:
## Live-In Positions

Accommodations are available for five live-in students at the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center. Students who are selected for live-in positions are expected to assist with daily feeding and cleaning, foaling and breeding, care of sick or injured horses, and other various tasks as assigned. Prior horse experience is necessary and previous involvement at the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is preferred. If you are interested in a live-in position, please email arabianhorse@cpp.edu or call (909) 869-4988.

title: Student Employment
content:
W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is preferred. If you are interested in a live-in position, please email arabianhorse@cpp.edu or call (909) 869-4988.

title: About Sunday Shows
content:
# About Sunday Shows

![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white-mobile.svg)
![](https://www.cpp.edu/common//green-and-gold/assets/images/linework-combo-offset-white.svg)

title: About Sunday Shows
content:
## A Tradition Started by W.K. Kellogg in 1926

The Sunday Show features Cal Poly Pomona students riding Arabians in different disciplines from Native Costume to Ladies Side Saddle. Additionally, the trick horse act is always a crowd favorite. Training and handling are accomplished entirely by the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center's students and staff, and the show program is designed to demonstrate the versatility and fine qualities of the Arabian breed.

![A student rides an Arabian horse in costume during a Sunday Show](../img/sunday-shows/gallery/sundayshow-1.jpg)
section: A Tradition Started by W.K. Kellogg in 1926

title: About Sunday Shows
content:
## About Sunday Shows

Weather permitting, the Sunday Shows are held on the first Sunday of the month at 2:00 PM. Shows are held in an outdoor arena, so rain any time the week before may affect scheduling. Please check this website or our [Facebook page](https://www.facebook.com/profile.php?id=100037191025956) for updates. For the safety of the students and horses, pets (except service animals) are not allowed.

title: TeachingHorse
content:
## Effective Leadership for an Uncertain Future

![TeachingHorse Facilitator Michelle Elrod and an Arabian Horse](../img/teachinghorse/equine-leadership-training-elrod.png)

Horses have been leading through uncertainty for millions of years. They use their roles within the herd to safely move the herd from one place to another. They have confidence in the roles of each leader and recognize that leadership must be shared. They do not know; they notice.

In these times of profound change and uncertainty, effective leaders who understand how to notice, set direction and pace for decisions and actions, and align their actions with their inner confidence will be successful in guiding organizations through change and into the future.

TeachingHorse, the premier provider of equine leadership training globally, and Cal Poly Pomona, known for our polytechnic “learn by doing” excellence, have partnered to provide world-class leadership development programs that define how leaders and teams will impact the future.

TeachingHorse at Cal Poly Pomona provides an innovative, experiential learning environment for leaders at all levels to become more agile, human-centered and future ready.
section: Effective Leadership for an Uncertain Future

title: TeachingHorse

* Enhanced Leadership Skills
* Stronger Team Culture
* Team Alignment
* Increased Productivity
* Reinvention of Leadership Development & Talent Management

title: TeachingHorse
content:
### Program Outcomes

* Practice noticing yourself, those around you and the environment
* Elevate agile decision making based on what you notice
* Move at a pace that reflects the current reality
* Align your inner and outer motivations to elevate confidence in your leadership

content:
# CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences

![Two year old Arabian fillies run in a Cal Poly Pomona pasture.](../img/teachinghorse/galloping-arabians1.png)

title: CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences
content:
## Learning Trails

\*Customized sessions available upon request.

title: CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences
content:
### Partnered Path - Executive Coaching

Develop your leadership skills with tailored coaching and a hands-on experiential session with horses to practice future-ready leadership. Virtual coaching followed by experiential learning at our Arabian Horse Center.

(1-2 Hours)

title: CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences
content:
### Herd Observation

Gain insights on shared leadership by observing herd dynamics and noticing at three levels. Collaborate with the team as you move amongst the herd and practice the four capabilities as you build trust with the herd of horses and with each other.

(3 Hours)

title: CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences
content:
### Herd Immersion

Explore leadership and team dynamics in a whole new way through the behavior of horses. Practice individual leadership skills, shared leadership team activities, guided discussions, and practical applications to become a future-ready team.

(Half Day)

title: CPP's TeachingHorse Experiences
content:
### Full Day Herd Immersion

Devote your team’s development to a full day of immersive experiences, unique activities with our Arabian horses, guided discussions, and exercises geared for team and individual leadership skills. Engage in deeply formative training experiences to prepare your team with future-ready principles and skills. The Full Day option includes flexible customization for your team.

content:

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content: # Leading Through Uncertainty

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content: ## Frequently Asked Questions

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What is TeachingHorse?

TeachingHorse is an equine-based leadership development program that uses the wisdom of horses to help humans lead through uncertainty with greater clarity, authenticity, and presence.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Who is this program for?

Leaders, teams, and professionals looking to build stronger communication, emotional intelligence, and decision-making skills.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Why horses?

Horses are masters of nonverbal communication, presence, and emotional regulation, having led sustainable, attuned communities for millions of years. Their ability to respond with clarity and intention makes them powerful partners in leadership development. Simply being in their presence creates a profound, embodied learning experience—anchoring insights not just in the mind, but in the body. Across centuries of shared history, horses have walked alongside humans as companions, workers, and teachers. Through their honesty and natural beauty, they reflect our highest potential, offering a mirror that fosters deep self-awareness and lasting transformation.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Do I need horse experience?

No! All activities are conducted on the ground with professional facilitators and horse specialists guiding the experience. You’ll be safe, supported, and surprised by how much you learn. Everything you do is a challenge by choice, meaning you get to choose how you experience the learning.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### How do I book a session?

We’re currently in our pilot phase of the program. You can reach out via email for a personal consultation. Email us at teachinghorse@cpp.edu to start the conversation. We’ll walk you through available packages, dates, and logistics.
section: How do I book a session?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What does a typical session look like?

Each session is designed with the individual and/or group in mind. Our sessions can be customized to meet the needs of our clients. All experiential sessions will give learners the opportunity to have individual and team interactions with the horse and/or herd. All experiential sessions begin in a classroom setting. In addition to the experiential options, we offer multi-day, virtual and classroom options. Since this program is customized based on the client's needs, it is best to reach out to us at teachinghorse@cpp.edu so we can best support your needs.
section: What does a typical session look like?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What should I wear for an in-person experience?

You will need to wear closed-toe shoes to the equine facility. More specifically, tennis shoes or hiking boots with no more than a 2-inch heel are recommended. Jeans and athletic wear are fine. No shorts. For warmer temperatures it is also recommended that you wear a hat, sunblock, sunglasses and for cooler temperatures you should bring a jacket and a hat or beanie. Gloves are appropriate as well.
section: What should I wear for an in-person experience?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Where does this happen?

In-person sessions are held at the W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center, a serene outdoor environment perfect for reflection, connection, and powerful breakthroughs. If the appropriate facilities and resources are available, we have the ability to travel to a location closer to you.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What if I am afraid of horses?

Safety is our top priority for both humans and horses. All activities are ground-based and facilitated by certified professionals. Participants receive a full safety briefing before engaging in any activities. With that being said, everything we do is “challenge by choice” meaning, you get to choose how you participate and interact with the horses.
section: What if I am afraid of horses?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What’s the cost?

Pricing varies depending on session length, location, group size, and customization needs. Reach out for a tailored quote—we’ll walk you through all the details.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What if the weather’s bad?

Sessions may be rescheduled in the event of rain or high winds. We’re currently working toward securing an enclosed arena for year-round comfort and consistency.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Is the program accessible?

We are committed to providing accessible options for all to participate and will continue improving accessibility further. Our facilitators are trained to provide accommodations to support a variety of needs. Please let us know about any accessibility requests in advance; we’re here to help you fully participate.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What is the Diamond Model of Shared Leadership™?

The Diamond Model is the core framework of TeachingHorse. Inspired by how herds of horses lead through uncertainty, it includes four essential leadership capabilities: Attention, Direction, Energy, and Congruence. These are practiced through roles such as leading from the front, middle, and behind—building agility, trust, and collective intelligence.
section: What is the Diamond Model of Shared Leadership™?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What leadership challenges does this help solve?

Whether you’re navigating change, leading through complexity, managing burnout, or building a new team culture—TeachingHorse provides experiential insight that helps you move from confusion to clarity. You’ll walk away with tools to improve communication, decision-making, self-awareness, and team alignment.
section: What leadership challenges does this help solve?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Can this be part of an existing leadership program or retreat?

Yes! TeachingHorse can be integrated into broader leadership initiatives. We offer both stand-alone experiences and modular formats that complement existing development programs, retreats, executive coaching, or campus initiatives. Ask us about customization options.
section: Can this be part of an existing leadership program or retreat?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Is this a team-building program?

It’s more than that. While TeachingHorse naturally strengthens trust and cohesion, the primary focus is leadership development. Participants gain personal insight and practical tools for shared leadership—far beyond typical icebreakers or morale boosters.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Do you offer virtual or hybrid options?

Yes, we offer a virtual curriculum built on the same principles of the Diamond Model. Virtual modules include storytelling, reflection, and peer coaching frameworks that reinforce the experiential learning, even without direct interaction with horses.
section: Do you offer virtual or hybrid options?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### What kind of organizations work with TeachingHorse?

We’ve worked with public agencies, universities, corporate teams, healthcare professionals, nonprofit leaders, and executive cohorts. Whether you're a senior leader, emerging professional, or cross-functional team, TeachingHorse helps bring clarity and enables agility in uncertain times.
section: What kind of organizations work with TeachingHorse?

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
content:
### Can I come visit the Arabian Horse Center?

Yes! The W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center is open to the public Monday - Friday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. In addition, we host monthly Sunday Shows where you will get to see some of our TeachingHorse herd members in action! [Visit About Sunday Shows](../sunday-shows/about-sunday-shows.shtml) for dates and times.
section: Can I come visit the Arabian Horse Center?

title: CP Fascinator
content:
# CP Fascinator

Fascinator is a younger mare who truly loves people. She is in training under saddle to hopefully be in our Sunday Shows and student lesson program down the road. Her calm nature has made her a favorite among students and she always loves to greet visitors over the fence.

title: CP Heir About Her
content:
## "Felicity"

Felicity loves people and her calm energy makes her a perfect partner for many of our hands on-activities with horses here at the AHC. She was bred and born here, went on to become a successful show horse, and now has returned to us to help teach the next generation of horse enthusiasts.

title: CP Regal Belle

Belle is a bay mare with white star and snip face markings. She is a patient and kind teacher and she loves her people. Belle is a big part of our Horses for Heroes program where the Arabian Horse Center partners with the Veterans Resource Center.

title: Meet the Team
content: ### Certified TeachingHorse Facilitators

title: Meet the Team
content:
## Facilitators

![Jesus Avalos](../img/teachinghorse/jesus.png)

title: Meet the Team
content:
### Jesús Ávalos

Jesús Ávalos is a learning and development professional with over 20 years of experience in communication and leadership development across both public and private sectors. At Cal Poly Pomona, he leads strategic learning and organizational effectiveness initiatives, supporting staff, faculty, and students through customized learning solutions and leadership coaching. He also serves as a trusted consultant and trainer of trainers, bringing his expertise to high-impact projects and professional development programs campus-wide.

![Maddi Ogburn](../img/teachinghorse/maddi.png)

title: Meet the Team
content:
### Maddi Ogburn

Maddi has been working with and around horses for over 15 years and currently holds the role of Coordinator of Horse Training and Farm Operations at Cal Poly Pomona’s W.K. Kellogg Arabian Horse Center. Her career with horses has brought Maddi across the country and around the world, working with many well regarded horse trainers and professionals. While horses are Maddi’s passion, her heart is for people, and this is what has led her to explore the world of leadership development through the eyes of horses.

![Michelle Elrod](../img/teachinghorse/michelle.png)

title: Meet the Team
content:
### Michelle Elrod

Michelle Elrod is a passionate facilitator and coach with over 15 years of experience supporting individuals and teams across the private and public sectors. She designs meaningful learning experiences that help people grow with purpose, navigate change, and achieve their goals. Through coaching and guided development, Michelle turns learning into action—building confidence, collaboration, and lasting impact. She also has a deep admiration for horses and values their unique ability to ground us, foster presence, and teach self-awareness.

title: One Team | One Goal | Student Success
content:
## 10-year Reaffirmation of Accreditation

In 2019, CPP received reaffirmation of accreditation for a period of 10 years, the maximum length of time offered by WSCUC. This represents a strong vote of confidence in the university’s strategic plan and commitment to continual improvement. “This designation is a reflection of our campus-wide commitment to student success and to being an institution that continually strives for excellence in all our endeavors. This achievement demonstrates that our strategic planning efforts, built over years of collaboration across our campus community, are making an impact and that our desire to continually improve serves as our institutional hallmark,” said University President Soraya M. Coley.

[CPP @ WSCUC](https://www.wscuc.org/institutions/california-state-polytechnic-university-pomona/%20)

![cpp standing letters ](img/cpp-standing-letters-angle-2.png)

title: Enjoy
content:
## Enjoy Your Life

Want to visit an art gallery? Enjoy fine dining? See a horse show or take in a theatrical performance? Picnic in a rose garden? You can do all of this and more without ever leaving campus. And when you do venture out, you will find mountains, beaches and deserts that are close enough for day trips or full-on adventures.Southern California is unrivaled in its array of cultural, recreational and entertainment options. Whatever your passion, hobby or epicurean interest, you will find it here.

![Beaches, Parks, and Recreation - sand and shoreline of a beach](img/enjoy/beach-parks-recreation-500x500.jpg)

title: Live
content:
## Live Your Life

Cal Poly Pomona is in the heart of Southern California, surrounded by cities and neighborhoods to fit virtually any budget or lifestyle. You will find vibrant, diverse communities rich in culture and history — downtown districts, suburbs, and even semi-rural enclaves. High-quality medical care options abound, as do excellent school districts. If you want to explore the area but leave your car in the garage, you can take a bike, train or bus — and if you want to get your steps in, the campus is the perfect location.

![Location and Housing -  colorful homes on a pretty neighborhood](img/live/location-housing-500x500.jpg)

title: Succeed
content:
## Succeed in Your Life

In the California State University System, the largest of its kind in the nation Cal Poly Pomona is a one-of-a-kind campus in a renowned university system that spans the state and serves as an engine of innovation and prosperity. We pride ourselves on our diversity, inclusiveness and accessibility, and we embrace those who choose to join our university community. This is a place where you have an opportunity to earn tenure, build a career and transform lives. Many of our students are the first in their family to attend college. Our students appreciate the benefits of a unique polytechnic education.

![Campus Diversity - Graduates excited during commencement](img/succeed/campus-diversity-500x500.jpg)
